It works great, and all my sites are indexed and crawlable with Google, Screaming Frog, and Ahrefs. If I need to make any changes, I update in Lovable, and it deploys to Github, then to Vercel automatically. I check Vercel aft... See more

Core 30 and "this is not slop" links to every page continue for each location. Yes core 30 x 13 states sounds like a LOT of content - but! don't worry. Watch your rank maps.

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/gbp-category-then-core-30-audit
2
Jack He
Dec '25 • SEO
GBP Category, Then Core 30 Audit?
I've been following along with the AI SEO Mastery Pro course and I just want to make sure I have this correct. Currently I'm up to How to Do Local AI SEO > Core 30 Content Audit.
Steps ->
1st Get GBP Category & Services
Then do the Core 30 Content Audit
Like
1
2 comments
6
Miles Hayat
🔥
• Dec '25
Hey Jack yep youre on the right track 1st step is always getting your full GBP structure like primary category, all secondary categories, and every service listed under each one. That becomes your roadmap for what pages need to exist then you run the core 30 gap analysis with screaming frog to see what pages you already have and whats missing. The gap analysis compares your current site against the GBP structure and tells you what needs to be built or optimized.
So yeah GBP first, then audit, youve got it right :)

Dennis Thomas

For service businesses, I would say minimal. People probably just want pricing and availability and contact info. You want to funnel them into a form or a call. You don’t really want them browsing. Think about what people might be looking for. Just my thoughts.

Like
2
2 comments
5
Mark Farrow
• Dec '25
It’s mentioned in the course as a nice-to-have, but it’s not strictly necessary to change all of your existing URLs and set up 301 redirects—especially if those pages are already indexed and performing. If this were a brand-new site or a fresh build, I’d absolutely recommend including the location in the URL structure from the start. At this stage, focusing on optimizing your H1s, title tags, on-page content, and internal linking will give you the majority of the local SEO benefit without the ri... See more
2
Reply
6
Miles Hayat
🔥
• Dec '25
Slug helps a little, but it’s not worth changing if the page is already indexed and performing id recommned focus on H1s, titles, and local relevance that gives you the real working only update the URL if you’re doing a full rebuild or the current slug is truly weak

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/neighborhood-pages

Neighborhood pages
Hello, I'm making the Neighborhood pages, but I see the content output for the content from Claude for every page is the same. I remember hearing this is ok, as the location is differet so it will read differently from Google, but I want to make sure, as I'm making several ones. May I have some help?
Like
0
4 comments
6
Miles Hayat
🔥
• Dec '25
Yes similar structure is fine, but if its literally the same content with just the location name swapped, that could be an issue. Google's pretty smart about catching that now.
Try to add atleast some unique details about each neighborhood like local landmarks, specific features, etc the location difference helps but Google still wants to see real unique value on each page.

secondary categories

"services we offer"
GBP landing page (up)

yes 2 different GBPs can share same address if theyre different industries, thats fine google just allows it as long as theyre actually different businesses and phone numbers should unique for each GBP but address can be shared and for citations just make sure business names and phone numbers are different and your good and google actualy cares that theyre actually separate businesses not just fake listings at same spot

Yep, what Miles said. If they're in the same, or similar, niche Google will almost always "suppress" so only one of them will ever rank for anything beyond a brand search
but if the categories are different / unrelated, they can both rank

Logo link is weaker than anchor text but still counts. The real question: Is the $500 for SEO or for networking?
For SEO alone, not worth it. You already have the directory link from membership.
For getting in front of 100 local business owners as a new business, that is a different calculation. Relationships turn into referrals and clients.
If you need the exposure, do it. Just do not justify it as an SEO play. =)
0
Reply


===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/service-schema-question
2
Parth Sharma
Dec '25 • SEO
service schema Question
For service schema, should I provide Claude with all of my secondary categories and services as well as the primary ones, then use its output directly in all of the the Service pages?
Like
1
4 comments
6
Miles Hayat
🔥
• Dec '25
yes you should add the services that you have mentioned on your landing page if you provide that service then yes give it to Claude for the schema just make sure whatever services your adding schema for are actually on the page then you are good to go.
2
Reply
2
Parth Sharma
• Dec '25
@Miles Hayat thank you
1
Reply
5
Elliot Berard
• Dec '25
@Parth Sharma Each service page gets schema for that specific service only. Do not put all your services schema on every page.
If it were me, I would give Claude the details for each individual page as you build it. Service schema on your "Water Heater Repair" page should only reference water heater repair, not all 30 of your services.
Organization schema goes on every page. LocalBusiness schema goes on your GBP landing page. Service schema is page-specific. =)
4
Reply
2
Parth Sharma
• Dec '25
@Elliot Berard that helps, I was putting the same service schema on every single page

Fix the fundamentals first. The reviews are not the problem. =)

Like
1
4 comments
6
Miles Hayat
🔥
• Dec '25
Hey Daniel, numbered citation thing keeps it cleaner when theres multiple links and makes it way easier to swap links later with out messing up the text flow and easyfor users to know about in numbering but if you want to do inline anchor text thats fine too just depends what your going for and mostly big sites like Wikipedia they also do numbered citations

Mark Farrow
• Dec '25
This is cool I’ve never done it this way but can see it could be useful. @Daniel Tenney same result either way.

5
Elliot Berard
• Dec '25
@Daniel Tenney Because the superscript is small, counts as a link, and we want people to stay on page. not following outbound links.

we don't want users to click on the links - they're there for Google

Elliot Berard

@Arlen Jenks I think about it even simpler than that (personally). Nav: Home, About, Contact. That is it.
No Services hub. No Serving Areas hub. Nothing else.
All your service and location linking happens through editorial body links in your content.
Homepage body content links to category pages. Category pages link to service pages.
Navigation links pass almost zero authority. Body links pass the juice. Since I'm ranking the GBP listing I'm not as worried about Nav bar structure vs. optimizing link... See more

Elliot Berard

@Daniel Tenney Case by case. Most developers know how to use multiple builders, or can figure it out in a half hour.
If they are not ranking, you COULD rebuild in your preferred stack if you want to do the extra work.
If they are ranking well, work in their existing platform. Do not touch it if it is ranking.
For devs, Upwork works fine. Find specialists for each platform as needed. =)

5
Elliot Berard
• Dec '25
@Nathan Gabriele It is understandably confusing, but services are not products and should not be mixed up in the GBP.
Products are things like hair brushes, or dogs, or used second hand pool noodles.
Services are like hair brushing, dog walking, or pool noodle flagellation.

we do it like this
/ann-arbor-mi/jackson-rd/car-wash
so basically /city/neighborhood/service structure that way each location gets its own unique url no canibalzation issue and it will clean across all locations just make sure the LocalBusiness schema on each page points to the right GBP and you're all set

5
Elliot Berard
• Dec '25
@Colton Hibbert First:
Congrats on the big win!!! How does it feel?! =)
Option 3 or Miles' structure both work. The key is unique URLs per location.
For 60+ GBPs with multiple in the same city:
Structure: /ann-arbor-jackson-rd/ as the GBP landing page, then /ann-arbor-jackson-rd/car-wash/ for services under that location.
Each location gets its own sub-folder. Services nest under their specific location.
LocalBusiness schema on each landing page points to that location's GBP.
Do not build one shared Co... See more

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/gbp-landing-page
1
Lesli Rose
Dec '25 • SEO
GBP Landing Page
Should I have a designated GBP landing page rather than link to the homepage of my website?
Like
0
4 comments
2
Daniel Tenney
• Dec '25
Does your business have multiple locations or are you also looking to rank for Organic search?
If no, your GBP landing page and Home page should be the same page/link.
1
Reply
1
Lesli Rose
• Dec '25
Hi, it’s my agency website, just one location, but not really relevant for local search so yes ranking nationally with organic search is a goal. I’d like more flexibility with my homepage too so I think I will build a separate GBP landing page. Thanks for your answer, much appreciated.
0
Reply
5
Elliot Berard
• Dec '25
@Lesli Rose For single location local businesses, homepage IS the GBP landing page. It is your most powerful URL.
But your situation is different. If you are targeting national organic rankings and local SEO is not the priority, a separate GBP landing page makes sense.
Keeps your homepage flexible for broader targeting while still having a proper landing page for your GBP.
Just make sure that GBP landing page has full NAP, LocalBusiness schema, and links to your services. =)
0
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Dec '25
as @Elliot Berard said - if you're trying to rank the homepage non locally, don't link the gbp to it. Linking hte gbp to a url is a pretty strong signal to Google that it's a local business =)
0
Reply
1

Elliot Berard

@Daniel Tenney here is the thing about multi-location sites: If your locations are thousands of miles apart (Florida, California, Texas), treat them almost like separate local SEO campaigns that happen to share a domain.
Each GBP needs its own landing page with full NAP, and the service pages under each location should target that specific city.
a1garage has massive domain authority carrying their thin location pages. Most businesses do not have that luxury.
For your plumber, I would build location... See more

If the competition is low enough that there's enough topical relevance for that location, we won't build a core 30
the rank map determines what we produce

Shareef Huddle
Dec '25 • SEO
SAB with or without address
Okay, I know that everything says, gotta have an address. I have been going back and forth for two months for a company that is brand new this year. He did not want to use his address. I went through the GBP Optimization and Core 30 and let him know that rankings would definitely change if we could use his address. He finally agreed and here is the results. Friday ran scan, Sunday added address, this morning, Monday, ran scan. I never really realized how much of a difference until now. The proof is in the pudding ;)

5
Elliot Berard
• Nov '25
@Marjohn Oviedo With the classic multi-locational site-structure, we do not create brand-level non-local service pages. =)
Multi-location structure is:
Each location gets its own landing page
Each location gets its own Core 30 service pages (Service + City)
Navigation stays minimal: Home, About, Contact
Brand-level service pages without location create duplicate content issues and dilute your local relevance signals. You are trying to rank GBPs, not brand pages.
The system is designed around topograph... See more

Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Dec '25
for highly competitive niches, we'll often create non-local copies of the core 30 for nav bar links. DOn't want somoene at the nyc office to click a nav bar link and end up on a geo page focused on miami

Actually, when it is well done ther should not be a ranking blow at all... in the contrary.. it should give him an uplift.

Assuming you're still getting paid - keep producing content. At some point, start giving the content to the guy who's doing the new site rather than posting on the current one
as a side note - a rank drop after launching a new site is very, very common so I'd be quite hesitant in launching a new site if the existing site is ranking well

@Daniel Namie To add on here: Don't stop producing content.

Retaining clients after articles are done

Like
1
4 comments
5
Aiden Hardy
• Nov '25
@Caleb Ulku @Elliot Berard
0
Reply
5
Elliot Berard
• Nov '25
@Aiden Hardy This is a great question. If we fire them, or they fire us, we just stop. We finish up the scope we have underway (which they paid for) and wind things down.
How to get them to continue to pay is much more nuanced. The easiest way to think about it is going to the gym: if you stop going you slowly atrophy strength and muscle mass. They won’t see detrimental effects right off, but they will feel it eventually.

4
Doug Page
• Nov '25
I think too that as their 'consultant' you need to understand their business and always be looking for additional profitable optimizations to work on to help them dominate further. Don't wait for them to give you directions on what to do next. Most local businesses can't afford to have you do everything right away. And more optimization work continues to get added to the to do list. Also look at complimentary services that are more recurring like rep. management. Reputation score and velocity ar... See more
1
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Nov '25
if they're ranked for their primary keyword across their entire service area with no hope of getting better, there are 3 options we take
1 - add more categories, services
2 - add another gbp
3 - enter a maintenance mode where you publish articles monthly for any dots that slipped out of the top 3

@Frank Apuzzo Hey! Thanks man, appreciate it! 🙏

4
Stefan Girndt
• Nov '25
From my perspective and what @Caleb Ulku is telling us, you need
a) a local tracking tool like Local Dominator or Lead Snap
b) a way to get high power citations which Lead Snap can do
c) Claude at least on the pro plan to help with content creation.
Semrush or ahref doesn't help for local SEO.
POP can help with optimizing content but isn't needed on every page.
GM everywhere can be good for analyzing a potential prospect and find out what other businesses in that niche and location are doing.

Troy Lunt

Core-30 primary category
Just a quick core-30 question- if the primary category is the H1 on the home page and the secondary categories are H2, where do the services under the primary category go? In other words, on the GBP, there are services listed under the primary category. For the secondary category pages, the services listed under that category on the GBP are listed as H2 tags on the category page. What do you do with the services listed on the GBP under the primary category?
Like
0
6 comments
3
Israel Saucedo
• Nov '25
Maybe my niche is kinda easy but I try not to complicate shit too much. This is how I do it.
3
Reply
1
Troy Lunt
• Nov '25
@Israel Saucedo Thanks!
0
Reply
5
Mark Farrow
• Nov '25
Hi Troy, this is covered in several spots in the class. Under the primary category, you want to only choose 2 to 3 key services. The other 7 to 10 you have to try to fit them under the other categories to be able to create pages for them. Caleb does this to reduce the number of outbound links off of the homepage. When you continue studying the class, it will become clearer. Going through the the weekly huddles has been really beneficial.

Stefan Girndt

Caleb Ulku
👑
• Nov '25
it's always wrong... doesn't agree with what screaming frog finds, doesn't agree with what the search console says is indexed, doesn't agree with manually counting them
this isn't somehting we do that requires exact accuracy - precisely because you can't get an exact number for the questin of "how many url's are indexed"
it fluctuates all the time and every source will report a different number

5
Elliot Berard
• Nov '25
@Aiden Hardy You are doing fine. Volatility means just that: there will be some initial fluctuations as Google recrawls, reindexes, and tests your new content out on its users.
Google likes to do things on its own timeline. My suggestion is to not check it more frequently than once a week. You don't want to be reactive, as you will always be reacting to the wrong thing. Once a week allows you to assess trends, not react to singular data points.
I do think you may have too many secondary categories that overlap. =)
0
Reply
5
Aiden Hardy
• Nov '25
@Elliot Berard Yeah, that does make sense its almost like google is trying to figure out what we do is it "internet marketing service" or is it the 9 other categories we had listed 😅 How many categories would you recommend for an SEO agency?
0
Reply
4
Doug Page
• Nov '25
I didn't see this mentioned above but also be careful when you do the scans. Ranking is highly affected by open hours. Scans should be timed to occur during opening hours and at approximately the same time if you want to do map comparisons.
1
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Nov '25
were you open for both scans? if you're closed the scans usually look terrible
beyond that, just keep an eye on it - daily fluctuation is pretty normal

4
Doug Page
• Nov '25
@Aiden Hardy Be careful about showing hours as 24/7 in a business where that this is not typically normal. IM type businesses around me have set hours. Doing the 24/7 could be looked at by Google as someone trying to game the system. These hours need to be when a human is around to answer.

@Lalita Sundari H1 only is used 1 time per page.... never use h1 more often... the structure is really important h1 > h2 > h3 > h4 .... for every site and content. I Use SEO Meta Copy Plugin (firefox) .... to check these things when I make quick structural site check.
2
Reply
View 5 more replies
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Nov '25
supporting content points to the page it's supporting based on the keyword you're working to improve rank position for
H1, H2, etc just mean different headings. H1 is the main heading for the page, H2 is subheadings, etc
every page should have an h1 - main heading - but only one main heading

Reply

Other example: for the keyword lymphatic massage, Athens, ga
I will link to the service: Lymphatic massage athens, ga?

Yo, so basically Google allows this - Hotel plus Restaurant/Bar at the same address need two separate profiles. It's literally in their own documentation.​ (https://support.google.com/business/answer/3038177?hl=en )
About a subdomain: Honestly, that would backfire. Subdomains get treated as separate websites by Google - so you won't get any domain authority passed from your hotel profile. Just use a subfolder instead, like yourhotel.com/restaurant instead of restaurant.yourhotel.com.​
What you alr... See more

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/designer-debates
5
Mark Farrow
Nov '25 • SEO
Designer Debates
I've been hyperlinking to service pages from homepage with contextual links but my designer wants to put a "learn more" or "View Service" link under the 50-75 words to assist with user experience. I get that BUT...If I only do it that way we lose the contextual value, and if I do it both ways that just adds additional links diluting each links power into the service page? Overthinking this, what are your thoughts?
Like
0
4 comments
3
Raphael Rechberger
• Nov '25
Hey Mark,
Not overthinking – solid question. Here's how I do it:
Keep the contextual link in the text. That's where the real anchor value lives.
Add the "View Service" button below the teaser. Make it link to the exact same URL. Google doesn't split juice on duplicate internal links to the same page.
If you insist on a "Read More" toggle: put the full text in the HTML from the start. No JS lazy-load. Use <details><summary> or CSS collapse without display:none. Crawlers see everything and dont lose c... See more
1
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Nov '25
So few people will ever click it that it doesn't really matter from a UX perspective
that being said, if the designer really wants a button, add a button. Also add the editorial in-content link, as @Raphael Rechberger suggested
1
Reply
5
Elliot Berard
• Nov '25
@Mark Farrow I'm preaching to the choir, but if it were me I would keep the contextual link. That’s where anchor value lives.
As Caleb says, add the button if the designer wants it. Make both point to the same URL.
The first anchor text typically carries the most weight for relevance, but all links consolidate authority to that destination.
Google doesn’t split juice on duplicate internal links. UX wins, SEO stays intact. Done. =)
1
Reply
5
Mark Farrow
• Nov '25
@Elliot Berard TY Elliot.
0
Reply
1




5
Designer Debates

@Iman Diaz Focus on local. That’s where the money is.

National is a long game. Only chase it if budget and patience are there. (I love national, personally).

Caleb Ulku
👑
• Nov '25
Yeah you can rank for everything, but it's going to be harder and harder to rank for secondary GBP categories that most GBPs have as the primary category
Separate GBP's could work at the same address, but Google usually doesn't "like" that if they're actually the same business
Generally I recommend choosing the primary category based on what the client wants calls for the most
You'll need A LOT of topical relevance for secondary categories that others have as primary

Doug Page
• Nov '25
I avoided Elementor and Divi for a number of years because of code bloat and only used the free version of Elementor when I was forced to by some other site buildering tools I was using. However, the latest version of Elementor is much cleaner and a lot easier to work with especially when getting into the deep details. Speed is excellent as well. But I still build sites with no extra builders when I only need the odd feature that I can easily use a much lighter plugin for. You really only need these builders for the additional frills if needed.

0
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Nov '25
"Massive website with ai slop" makes me nervous
Google uses entity based search
The goal is to connect your business (entity) with your service (entity) and location (entity)
Posts about whether cats can eat lettuce fudge this up
Very large sites that aren't ranking well are clients where we start by deleting a massive amount of content first
More content won't help sites with hundreds of indexed pages rank better
Their issue is almost always the wrong content
1
Reply
1




3
Honest feedback please


===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/question-about-geographical-relevance
1
Chris Nolan
Nov '25 • SEO
Question About Geographical Relevance
Hey @Caleb Ulku if I have a client who only works from their office, such as dentist, doctor or lawyer and they don't physically go to customers in their service area like a plumber would, should I still be creating geographic relevance blog posts, or do I skip those and just build topical relevance once I have my core 30? Does this SEO process still work for these types of businesses?
Like
0
4 comments
4
Stefan Girndt
• Nov '25
The process will be the same and you should build local relevance as long as you don't rank 1-3 in the areas people live who will drive to your client's office or practice.
Especially for medical professionals, people will drive a long way to get to a good one.
That might be different for restaurants or gyms.
1
Reply
5
Elliot Berard
• Nov '25
@Chris Nolan You still will build geographically relevant blog posts once your rank map is the 30-40% green.
You may be known for dentist, but that doesn't mean Google associates you with your specific area.
1
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Nov '25
Yep you still need goegraphic relevance. Instead of the content being "hey google we love going to this location for our customers and here's driving directions our staff uses" the content is more "hey google people who live in this area love coming and visitng our office and here's driving directions they take"

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/core-30-for-agency-website-or-not-plus-sab-query
2
Mike Ru
Nov '25 • General discussion
Core 30 for Agency website or not plus SAB query
I think i know the answer but wanted to check with the community. For a marketing agency, are we basically following the Core 30? And what you guys think about SAB no address for that. Otherwise your limiting yourself to a local area which if your living in the middle of nowhere in some one horse town wont get a whole lot of business. @Caleb Ulku
Like
0
2 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Nov '25
No address is very hard to rank, as I've said many times. If you want to rank it, you need an address
There is no difference in proximity / reach whether you show an address or not except not showing an address is harder to rank
Google still knows where your GBP was verified and will still care very much about proximity to its location. The service areas section on the GBP should be filled out (because every box should be filled out) but it's largely meaningless as a rank factor
1
Reply
2
Mike Ru
• Nov '25
OK, that makes it a no brainer decision then. Thanks for clearing that up.
0
Reply
1




2
Core 30 for Agency website or not plus SAB query

User experience & Core 30 links
For lots of home service businesses the nav bar usually has a link to a services page. I think lots of users and clients would expect this. For Core 30 we create category service pages linked in the body of the homepage. According to the training, links in the nav bar are weak and would reduce the power of that same link found later down the page, so does that means you don't want a link in the nav bar to one of your core 30 pages? Would you create a general services page linked to from that nav... See more

Caleb Ulku

Keep in mind that also according to the trainign I say that you aren't trying to rank individual URLs. They all exist for relevance to the GBP so this reduction in strength to specific urls is much less important for local than it is for non local
We just try to keep the nav bar and footers small. Beyond that, do waht makes sense for you, your client, your site visitors.

No shows were an issue with the campaign we ran and honestly with any campaign that books calls from a single fb ad

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/link-structure
2
Rachael VanWagner
Nov '25 • SEO
Link Structure
Is it okay to link from a main service page under one category to a separate category page? I made a quick diagram to make this question easier to answer. Is the purple line linking from subservice 4 to the Category 2 page okay, or will it harm my SEO?
Like
0
3 comments
5
Mark Farrow
• Nov '25
In the class he says specifically not to do this. If the pages are similar and related thats ok leave them where they are as they contribute to topical relevance but don't interlink them. Remember to not worry about Dup content or competing urls as we aren't trying to rank URLs we are raking GBP's.
0
Reply
2
Rachael VanWagner
• Nov '25
@Mark Farrow Thank you! I thought the answer would be no, and I wanted to be sure before I break my brain coming up with a workaround. It's quite a mind-shift from standard SEO, and I appreciate you taking the time to steer me in the right direction.
1
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Nov '25
No you should not do this. Each category should be seen as its own topical hierarchy. Interlinking them confuses google because google is just stupid math and pattern recognition
1
Reply
1




2
Link Structure

Reply
5
Elliot Berard
• Nov '25
@Brady Haynes
Here’s the layout:
H1 = exact match keyword (e.g. “Water Heater Replacement Houston”)
H2s = lots of them, especially rewritten PAA questions
Text blocks = 50–75 words per section, answer first sentence
Images = 5–9 per article, sticky side images work great
Rich text = yes, use callouts, bold, lists, etc.
Category vs Service vs Product vs Location:
Category: 1 page per GBP category, linked from homepage
Service: 1 page per GBP service, 2–3 high-value ones linked from homepage
Product: Same s... See more
2
Reply
1
Brady Haynes
• Nov '25
@Elliot Berard Thanks Elliot ,yes, I'm creating template pages that I can duplicate page structure exactly as is, an populate with all of the content, swap out pics. Much appreciated

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/gbp-major-changes-no-trigger-for-re-verification
5
Mark Farrow
Oct '25 • SEO
GBP Major Changes - no trigger for re-verification
I took a client that needed a new website rebrand as he was leaving a franchise, so perfect timing to create a fresh website with Core 30. On the GBP we changed the primary category and removed one and added two additional. We changed the name, logo, the website URL, product card Url's, the booking link, removed some recent posts. AND all this triggered NO VERIFICATION. 🤷‍♂️ I made the changes on the front end of the GBP not thru the Lead Snap API.
This was a service based GBP, and many competit... See more

Keep the same location and simply make the address visible, or

From an SEO standpoint, what would be smarter — short-term and long... See more

2
7 comments
5
Elliot Berard
• Oct '25
@Yamil Abdol kader
Short term: Make your address visible and capitalize on the work you've already done
Long term: Get a second GBP (renting an office for instance) and then build out a second Core 30 at this new location.
1
Reply
1
Yamil Abdol kader
• Oct '25 (edited)
Thanks.
I have two different scenarios I’d like some feedback on.
Scenario 1
The homepage (landing page) is targeting a different city than the actual business address city.
I’m assuming that changing the city on that homepage could hurt the GBP rankings it already has — even though the address is hidden and I’m not getting many calls from GBP right now.

My current thought ... See more
1
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Oct '25
Google knows where the gbp was created even with the address hidden. Whether the address is visible or not, ranking outside the city will be a challenge especially in the situation where the gbp is in a suburb and you want to rank in the primary city
0
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Oct '25
Adding an address different from the one the gbp was originally verified with very likely triggers a reverification
So in most scenarios, the best thing to do is what @Elliot Berard suggested. Create a second GBP as you'll need access to the address to re-verify the first one anyhow

Caleb Ulku

That image was cropped or something?
Anyhow - virtual offices
TL;DR they can work, but they're a challenge. Only so many GBP's can be verified at the same address and a lot of virtual offices are already over that amount
Additionally, it will open yourself up to a suspension - especially if you start ranking and your competitors tattle that your location isn't your location

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/issue-changing-categories-for-my-client
1
Eric Aumen
Oct '25 • SEO
Issue changing categories for my client
My HVAC client has Air Conditioning Contractor set as his primary category and wants me to get him more furnace repair work. My plan was to make HVAC contractor his primary category so we dont have to switch categories seasonally, but the changes will not stick in google. I have tried 5+ times on Google and a handful of times via Leadsnap and they always revert back to the old settings prior to my changes. Any helpful insight?
Like
0
5 comments
5
Elliot Berard
• Oct '25 (edited)
@Eric Aumen
Yeah, that happens sometimes. When my GBP keeps reverting, and if he isn't ranking at all, I would:
Edit in Incognito – Use a clean browser session.
Remove all other categories first – Then save. Wait 24h. Then add the new primary.
Check for conflicting edits – If someone else has access (client, agency), they might be overwriting you.
Suspicious activity – Too many edits in a short time can trigger reverts. Wait a few days before retrying.
Use Support – If it keeps reverting, contact GBP support and explain. They can manually approve.
1
Reply
1
Eric Aumen
• Oct '25
@Elliot Berard So you delete all categories except the current primary (which needs to be changed). Then save and wait 24 hours. Then delete the current primary which is wrong and add the new primary? Then a couple days later add the other categories and services?
0
Reply
5
Elliot Berard
• Oct '25
@Eric Aumen Only if the GBP isn't ranking at all. Don't touch things that are ranking.
Otherwise, skip that step and go straight to support.
0
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Oct '25
Just keep making the change. It will stick eventually
Google reviews all changes and this is just a sign that Google disagrees with your change. Eventually it will stop changing it back though. This is very common
1
Reply
1
Eric Aumen
• Oct '25
@Caleb Ulku Thank you!
0
Reply

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/value-of-gbp-for-a-national-site
1
David Kowal
Oct '25 • General discussion
Value of GBP for a national site
Hi, I'm in discussions with a potential new client based near Birmingham in the UK. They are able to service about a quarter of England but have longer term ambitions to spread further.
If we have a GBP near Birmingham will is work against us when trying to rank in Liverpool or Manchester, or as long as these are listed in the service area covered - would be better to have a GBP, rather than not have one?
Hope the question makes sense, I'd appreciate a steer on this one. Thank you.
Like
0
4 comments
5
Elliot Berard
• Oct '25
@David Kowal It will not work against you to have multiple Google Business profiles.
For national businesses, international businesses, and worldwide websites Part of the game is knowledge consistency across the Internet.
Each GBP will have its own Core 30 structure underneath it., building up local relevancy and authority.
1
Reply
1
David Kowal
• Oct '25
@Elliot Berard thanks Elliot appreciate your feedback 👍
1
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Oct '25
If you're trying to rank the homepage non-locally, don't use it as a GBP landing page. For obvious reasons, a GBP landing page sends a pretty strong signal that you're trying to rank locally
Otherwise, yes it's almost always better to have a GBP even for a non local business. Improves E-E-A-T
0
Reply
1
David Kowal
• Oct '25
Thanks Caleb

4
Matthew Furman
• Oct '25
@Justin Bryce Here are your 3 phases to follow:
1) Build out the core 30 (GBP & website landing page - prob Homepage)
2) Check Rank Map - if all red you need Topical Relevance (PAA questions in the FAQ section linked to articles). Write 3 per month, and run your Rank Map. When you get 1/3 green, you're good with Topical relevance, move to Geographic Relevance.
3) Go after 4s, 5s, and 6s. Build your Landmark pages, put a link for each location on a dedicated Locations page which you link in the Nav ... See more

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/website-rankings-improving-but-gbp-radius-not-growing-what-could-be-missing
1
Jaime Javier Ruiz de Castro
Oct '25 • SEO
Website rankings improving, but GBP radius not growing, what could be missing?
Hey @Caleb Ulku ,
I wanted to share a quick update and get your thoughts. For one of our client projects, we’ve implemented almost all of the Core 30 framework — including supporting content, geolocal content, and the full site structure. We’ve followed your process very closely.
The good news is that the website is performing better organically in the SERPs, and rankings are moving up nicely. However, we’re not seeing the same level of growth in the Google Business Profile radius — it still seems... See more
Like
1
3 comments
5
Elliot Berard
• Oct '25
@Jaime Javier Ruiz de Castro Website ranking but GBP radius not expanding usually means you need real authority links.
PBN links prevent AI slop but do not expand GBP radius. You need Chamber memberships and youth sports sponsorships for actual geographic expansion.
A few questions:
What links have you sourced, and are any pages having issues being indexed?
What does the rank map show?
Is the location in a particularly competitive area?
1
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Oct '25
Yeah I'd have the same questions as Elliot.
I'd also add questions around the GBP optimization and activity. We don't track organic rankings so I'll ignore that for the most part. If Core 30 has been implemented with limited progress on the rank map, it's usually due to a list of external links.
Do you have before / after rank maps? Are you adding the external authority links in your content (that's an easy one to skip)? Is the content you're publishing indexing? Are you sourcing links to them? Are you souricng other high trust links?
1
Reply
1
Jaime Javier Ruiz de Castro
• Oct '25
Thank you to both for taking the time to FU my question.
Locally, we don't have the exact suggestions to get local links, but thanks to your comments I was able to research chatGPT and it seems there is a turn around and get local. Have a great weekend

4
Matthew Furman
• Oct '25
@Justin Bryce Here are your 3 phases to follow:
1) Build out the core 30 (GBP & website landing page - prob Homepage)
2) Check Rank Map - if all red you need Topical Relevance (PAA questions in the FAQ section linked to articles). Write 3 per month, and run your Rank Map. When you get 1/3 green, you're good with Topical relevance, move to Geographic Relevance.
3) Go after 4s, 5s, and 6s. Build your Landmark pages, put a link for each location on a dedicated Locations page which you link in the Nav ... See more

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/website-rankings-improving-but-gbp-radius-not-growing-what-could-be-missing
1
Jaime Javier Ruiz de Castro
Oct '25 • SEO
Website rankings improving, but GBP radius not growing, what could be missing?
Hey @Caleb Ulku ,
I wanted to share a quick update and get your thoughts. For one of our client projects, we’ve implemented almost all of the Core 30 framework — including supporting content, geolocal content, and the full site structure. We’ve followed your process very closely.
The good news is that the website is performing better organically in the SERPs, and rankings are moving up nicely. However, we’re not seeing the same level of growth in the Google Business Profile radius — it still seems... See more
Like
1
3 comments
5
Elliot Berard
• Oct '25
@Jaime Javier Ruiz de Castro Website ranking but GBP radius not expanding usually means you need real authority links.
PBN links prevent AI slop but do not expand GBP radius. You need Chamber memberships and youth sports sponsorships for actual geographic expansion.
A few questions:
What links have you sourced, and are any pages having issues being indexed?
What does the rank map show?
Is the location in a particularly competitive area?
1
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Oct '25
Yeah I'd have the same questions as Elliot.
I'd also add questions around the GBP optimization and activity. We don't track organic rankings so I'll ignore that for the most part. If Core 30 has been implemented with limited progress on the rank map, it's usually due to a list of external links.
Do you have before / after rank maps? Are you adding the external authority links in your content (that's an easy one to skip)? Is the content you're publishing indexing? Are you sourcing links to them? Are you souricng other high trust links?
1
Reply
1
Jaime Javier Ruiz de Castro
• Oct '25
Thank you to both for taking the time to FU my question.
Locally, we don't have the exact suggestions to get local links, but thanks to your comments I was able to research chatGPT and it seems there is a turn around and get local. Have a great weekend

Caleb Ulku

• Oct '25
If you're building geo relevance, we'll geotag with coordinates that match the dot we're after (assuming it's a photo of people at work)
for topical relevance we'll usually tag the business address

I have a new client who is in the Drain, Septic & Sewer space. We've done all the regular optimizations and the maps rankings are steadily improving - well, all the maps rankings that have to do with drain, septic or sewer are increasing.
One of the main things the client wanted was to rank for "slinger truck" in Google Maps. I personally don't think Google will recognize them for such a different service when everything about the company is about drainage.
I'd love to hear other people's real wor... See more

Caleb Ulku

• Oct '25
It's possible, but gets more difficult to compete with specialized providers. Keep all the topical silos separate to build relevance on each topic

• Oct '25
@Caleb Ulku Ya most of the competitors are "so and so slinger truck services" so it has been difficult indeed. Thanks for weighing in
0
Reply
5
Elliot Berard
• Oct '25
@Reese Richards If slinger truck is a real service they offer, add it to GBP and create matching page. Should not hurt existing rankings. =)
Google understands businesses offer multiple services. Drain company can also offer slinger truck services.
Add it as GBP service, create "Slinger Truck [City]" page, get external link to it. Build topical relevance around that service separately.
Focus on Core 30 first for main services. Then add slinger truck content. Rankings in different service categories... See more

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/home-page-title-for-multiple-gbp-categories
1
Sean Parry
Oct '25 • SEO
Home page title for multiple GBP categories

Elliot Berard
• Sep '25
@Jon Burden To dive into this a bit more: There is a LOT going on with this website.
Every cool fade in is some Javascript making the website do neat things. Unfortunately, if I middle click my mouse button and scroll up/down I find that the page catches as it has to re-load the JS.
Now, if you go to "don't allow Javascript" in your browser settings, your entire website disappears. It looks like much of it is served using JS pulling information from other places.

Elliot Berard
• Oct '25 (edited)
@Muhammad Abdullah There are many online schema generators that will assist you with this as well.
The goal is to have the schema on page, the delivery method doesn't matter. RankMath is an excellent plugin, but if you have duplicate schema, then remove one of the instances of it.

Matthew Furman
• Oct '25
Hi Muhammed. I use RankMath also and had some issues with schema. I disabled it for all but article schema on posts. Generate your schema through AI and add as a custom HTML block to the pages. The schema you generate is way more effective, and the more types, as long as they pertain, the better.

View 2 more replies
5
Elliot Berard
• Sep '25
@Ron Kwok I'm going to agree with what @Stefan Girndt said: Having links to the chambers and associations is going to divide the authority your pages pass on.
If the pages aren't being indexed, reach out to the associations and ask for correction. This is odd.
Reach out to Leadsnap support to ask what is going on. They will either be able to tell you or potentially figure it out for you.
You can have the links in plain text, but I wouldn't have done so myself. Naked plaintext URLs are not the prettiest things, and Google won't index them as anything but text.
0
Reply
5
Elliot Berard
• Oct '25 (edited)
@Ron Kwok @Stefan Girndt I overthought this greatly.
I've though about this a bit more, and while this is an interesting point from a theoretical SEO standpoint... it honestly doesn't matter.
Those links in your Footer will NOT be the difference between being on the first page of the SERPs or having your GBP in the map pack.
I retract my advice. It isn't worth thinking about vs. creating more content and working on the process.

Brandon M
• Oct '25
@Ron Kwok Upon my initial review of what I would catch, their Primary Category is Beauty School, with their main first subcategory being Beauty Salon. You are showing up as Massage School as your subcategory, which could create a conflict. Also, what is the overall difference in the total number of reviews between you and Aveda? If that difference is a wide margin, it could already have extra staying power.

2
Ron Kwok
• Oct '25

Reply
5
Elliot Berard
• Oct '25
@Ron Kwok There is not a checklist because it doesn't matter what the competitors are doing.
Stop analyzing competitors. Rank map tells you what is working. =)
For local links - search "[your city] chamber of commerce," "[your city] youth sports," "[your city] nonprofits." Contact them about sponsorships or memberships. That is how you get authority links.
If massage program is a real service you offer, keep the category. Reviews look good at 4.8 stars.

Get your categories and services correct. Build the relevance as is in the training and you'll outrank them
I have spent way too long trying to analyze what competition is doing with no clear answers or path forward. So we don... See more

For 2 days now I'm trying to change the address of my client's GBP.
I made a post on the website, their FB page and their GBP last week about the move and updated the website content accordingly to fit the new address.
But when I change the address in the GBP, the address reverts back each time, even after they submitted the video verification at their new address.
So what's the deal here? Has anybody encountered this behaviour before?

Why are you changing the GBP address if they've already been verified for the original location? Why not add a second location?

Brandon M
• Oct '25
@Stefan Girndt be very careful on changes, if you keep trying multiple times, they may think you are hacking the account and could suspend the page. Caleb Ulku
👑
• Oct '25
This is common. Google will often decide you're wrong when you make edits to the GBP and just change it back
One solution is to just keep on making the change, over and over again. Eventually you'll either trigger a re-verification or the edit will stick

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/too-many-clients-only-3-gbp-map-pack-spots-how-do-you-handle-it
4
Karan Holland
Oct '25 • General discussion
Too many clients, only 3 GBP map pack spots — how do you handle it?
If you run monthly Local SEO for multiple clients in the same city + same service, how do you deal with the 3-slot map pack?
Example: Christchurch electricians — I could sign 5 solid clients. For “electrician Christchurch,” only 3 can show in the map pack at once. How do you keep it fair and clear?
Like
0
4 comments
3
Brandon M
• Oct '25
@Karan Holland if you do that you are competing against yourself, you only want to be representing one of the clients and then move on to another city. There is only one #1 spot, if you got all the clients in the #1,#2, and #3 spot, or the top 5 spots, you have to sacrifice others and can never get them where they want to be. Make sense?
0
Reply
4
Karan Holland
• Oct '25
@Brandon M Christchurch only has 3 map-pack slots. To keep this fair and ROI-driven, I offer two paths:
Exclusive City (you’re the only brand I push for ‘Electrician Christchurch’ as the primary city) or Non-Exclusive (we split by suburbs/services and judge success on leads and calls). Most tradies start non-exclusive at $300/mo and add suburb/service boosters. If you want exclusivity, it’s $____/mo due to scarcity.

@Jon Burden To dive into this a bit more: There is a LOT going on with this website.

Now, if you go to "don't allow Javascript" in your browser settings, your entire website disappears. It looks like much of it is served using JS pulling information from other places.

@Muhammad Abdullah There are many online schema generators that will assist you with this as well.

Hi Muhammed. I use RankMath also and had some issues with schema. I disabled it for all but article schema on posts. Generate your schema through AI and add as a custom HTML block to the pages. The schema you generate is way more effective, and the more types, as long as they pertain, the better.

Elliot Berard
• Sep '25
@Ron Kwok I'm going to agree with what @Stefan Girndt said: Having links to the chambers and associations is going to divide the authority your pages pass on.
If the pages aren't being indexed, reach out to the associations and ask for correction. This is odd.
Reach out to Leadsnap support to ask what is going on. They will either be able to tell you or potentially figure it out for you.
You can have the links in plain text, but I wouldn't have done so myself. Naked plaintext URLs are not the prettiest things, and Google won't index them as anything but text.

Elliot Berard
• Oct '25 (edited)
@Ron Kwok @Stefan Girndt I overthought this greatly.
I've though about this a bit more, and while this is an interesting point from a theoretical SEO standpoint... it honestly doesn't matter.
Those links in your Footer will NOT be the difference between being on the first page of the SERPs or having your GBP in the map pack.
I retract my advice. It isn't worth thinking about vs. creating more content and working on the process.

5
Elliot Berard
• Oct '25
@Ron Kwok There is not a checklist because it doesn't matter what the competitors are doing.
Stop analyzing competitors. Rank map tells you what is working. =)
For local links - search "[your city] chamber of commerce," "[your city] youth sports," "[your city] nonprofits." Contact them about sponsorships or memberships. That is how you get authority links.
If massage program is a real service you offer, keep the category. Reviews look good at 4.8 stars.

Get your categories and services correct. Build the relevance as is in the training and you'll outrank them

Doug Page

1
Reply
5
Mark Farrow
• Sep '25
The more you look at the material the clearer this becomes. I encourage watching the live Tuesday call recordings.
Primary Cat - homepage
Secondary Cats- silos
Services that fall under each silo.
Homepage links down to Secondary Cats and 2-3 key services.
Secondary Category Pages link down to each service.
Individual service pages link up to their secondary category page.
Service pages in the silo link to each other but not out to other silos.

City pages, like orlando, tampa, etc can largely be skipped. Google rolled out an update in August '25 to treat them as spam
Beyond that, we usually will ask AI to sort the services to the most semantically related category and make sure it knows the goal is local seo and ranking the GBP

2 comments

Brandon M
• Oct '25

0
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Oct '25
Fluctuations like this is ppretty normal. We run weekly rank maps and generally make planning decsisions on the best looking one from the prior month. If it keeps on like it is, then that's generally a sign that one of your competitors got serious or joined the chamber or something. Not enough of a drop to be a spam or algo update issue
If the new map is the best one, then I'd go back to core 30 content and sourcing local powerful links
1
Reply
1

This is common. Google will often decide you're wrong when you make edits to the GBP and just change it back
One solution is to just keep on making the change, over and over again. Eventually you'll either trigger a re-verification or the edit will stick

If you run monthly Local SEO for multiple clients in the same city + same service, how do you deal with the 3-slot map pack?
Example: Christchurch electricians — I could sign 5 solid clients. For “electrician Christchurch,” only 3 can show in the map pack at once. How do you keep it fair and clear?
Like
0
4 comments
3
Brandon M
• Oct '25
@Karan Holland if you do that you are competing against yourself, you only want to be representing one of the clients and then move on to another city. There is only one #1 spot, if you got all the clients in the #1,#2, and #3 spot, or the top 5 spots, you have to sacrifice others and can never get them where they want to be. Make sense?
0
Reply
4
Karan Holland
• Oct '25
@Brandon M Christchurch only has 3 map-pack slots. To keep this fair and ROI-driven, I offer two paths:
Exclusive City (you’re the only brand I push for ‘Electrician Christchurch’ as the primary city) or Non-Exclusive (we split by suburbs/services and judge success on leads and calls). Most tradies start non-exclusive at $300/mo and add suburb/service boosters. If you want exclusivity, it’s $____/mo due to scarcity.

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/need-help-with-core-30-gbp-category-and-services-organization

Need help with Core 30 - GBP Category and Services Organization
Hello Everyone! I'm new to the group and hoping I can get some help! I'm structuring the Core 30 and I am a bit stuck. I understand the Plumber example, but our business is not as straight forward. Our Primary Category (Real Estate Consultant), which is what 99% of our competitors are using, does not really explain our services as a "we buy houses" company. I added Secondary Category (Property Investment Company) since I saw a few of our competitors having. But the main issue is how do I organiz... See more
GBP Landing Page Diagram CHB.pdf
PDF • 79kb
Like
2
9 comments
4
Doug Page
• Sep '25
As I have no GBP knowledge in this area, I tried the following prompt in ChatGPT and it gave a pretty good summary of what to and not to use based on the services provided.
"What are the possible google business profile categories for a company the buys and sells houses. Also indicate under each category the possible services for each."
1
Reply
5
Mark Farrow
• Sep '25
The more you look at the material the clearer this becomes. I encourage watching the live Tuesday call recordings.
Primary Cat - homepage
Secondary Cats- silos
Services that fall under each silo.
Homepage links down to Secondary Cats and 2-3 key services.
Secondary Category Pages link down to each service.
Individual service pages link up to their secondary category page.
Service pages in the silo link to each other but not out to other silos.
0
Reply
1
Dee Garcia
• Sep '25

0
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
City pages, like orlando, tampa, etc can largely be skipped. Google rolled out an update in August '25 to treat them as spam
Beyond that, we usually will ask AI to sort the services to the most semantically related category and make sure it knows the goal is local seo and ranking the GBP

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/how-many-images-should-a-page-have
1
Austin Marland
Sep '25 • General discussion
How many images should a page have?
Is there a guideline for image best practices? Should every page have an image for each section to break up text?
Like
1
3 comments
5
Elliot Berard
• Sep '25
@Austin Marland One image per page minimum. More if it helps user experience, but do not overthink this.
Images are for users, not rankings. Focus on making pages easy to read and visually appealing.
Geotagged images help local SEO if you upload them to GBP, but regular website images are not a major ranking factor.
Write good content first, add images to break up text second. Images take two minutes to do and help. =)
2
Reply
1
Austin Marland
• Sep '25
@Elliot Berard Thanks
0
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
Usually we do minimum one image with additional images every few hundred words. Not exact. But if you only have a hero image and a wall of text, it’s going to be rather difficult to get google to index the content
1
Reply
1




1
How many images should a page have?

Caleb's apporach is Local first.

For obvious reasons, linking a GBP to the homepage sends a strong signal that it's a local site

Luan Nguyen

top 3 within one zipcode
If I bring 3 same industry business under same zipcode, is it possible top get all 3 of them ranking top 1-3? How is the proximity will effect the ranking? for example, downtown Dallas, if each zipcode get 3 businesses with same industry to maximize their GBP, core 30, etc, will it be too much competition against each other? The keyword will be "near me"
Like
1
9 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
I'm not sure what you're asking
You own 3 GBP's in the same zipcode and want all of them in the top 3?
Sure, possible. Very unlikely if they're linked to the same domain or have a clear footprint as Google usually won't put the same business twice in the top 3
1
Reply
1
Luan Nguyen
• Sep '25 (edited)
@Caleb Ulku for example, I acquire 3 different businesses but within same zipcode, and they are all hair salons, is it possible to get them all rank 1-3 on Google with the key word "near me" despite how small the zipcode area?
0
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
@Luan Nguyen I don't take on more than one client targeting the same category in the same geographic area
2
Reply
View 1 more reply
5
Elliot Berard
• Sep '25
@Luan Nguyen Google will not put 3 hair salons owned by the same person in the top 3. Their algorithm filters this automatically.
Even if you hide ownership, proximity makes this nearly impossible. "Near me" searches prioritize closest results - all 3 would have similar proximity scores. Furhter, Google is the world's largest data scraper, it would figure it out.
Much better strategy - pick one salon, dominate that market completely, then expand to different zip codes.
Don't compete against yourself. Focus on one business and crush it. =)
2
Reply
1
Luan Nguyen
• Sep '25
@Elliot Berard thank you for the insight. They are not same owners. Different owners but same category is what I was focusing on
0
Reply
5
Elliot Berard
• Sep '25
@Luan Nguyen If they are all different owners, and are all in the same zip code and are competition with each other, then I can't give advice other than to treat them as different engagements.
That said, I find this to be duplicitous and I don't take on more than one client within the same vertical.
I can't imagine what it would feel like to find out that your competitor is beating you while using the same agency, and I would worry about being sued for both lack of candor and conflict of interest.

Elliot Berard
• Sep '25
@Dean Ouellette
You are mixing up Phase 1 and Phase 3. =)
Core 30 (Phase 1) = One page per GBP service for your primary city only:
Pool heater repair Phoenix
Pool heater replacement Phoenix
Pool pump repair Phoenix
Pool pump replacement Phoenix
That gives you ~30-40 pages total matching your GBP services.
Phase 3 (Geographic Relevance) = Adding other cities like Tempe, Arcadia, Scottsdale.
Only do Phase 3 if your rank map shows gaps after Core 30 is complete.
Most businesses rank everywhere before finishi... See more

Like
0
3 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
Is another number showing? It's common that Google won't accept updates to the GBP
When you make the update, Google decides if it agrees or not. If not, then it won't update
If you link the GBP with a management software, it can force it to accept the update
Otherwise, just keep updating and checking and updating... eventually it will stick

@Caleb Ulku
Like
0
3 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
Rarely do we do anything other than the city the GBP is located in. The ones that we do are based on local searches. For example, Long Island, NY has few searches with the city name - county is more prevalent there. Same thing for Orange County, CA and some parts of the Jersey Shore
I'd also check rank maps of your competitors to see how they're ranking across cities

Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
I would look at competitor rank maps and see if any of them are able to rank in the larger city. No reason to guess. Every competitor would rather rank in the large city. If none have done it, your guy probably won't be the first
The answer is usually no, but I've been surprised sometimes

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/category-and-service-page-images

Dale Martin
Sep '25 • SEO
Category and Service Page Images
Hi there gang, What is the best way to add images to our category and service pages? How are you generating those images? Do you use the category or service keyword + the location in the naming of the image? Is there a particular size the image needs to be?
Like
0
2 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
AI generation
Keyword rich naming conventions stopped moving the needle when Google stopping crawling source code
Image size needs to be correct to not have your website builder resize it. It should be uploaded in a compressed version at the correct size it will be used. Do NOT resize an image with a website builder. Re-upload it with a different size

Ron Kwok
Sep '25 • SEO
Which should I trust: Trends.google.com or Lead Snap
When I look at Leadsnap, it tells me that "Nail Tech School" has higher search volume than "Nail School". However, Trends.Google tells me the opposite. My hunch is to trust Trends.Google more since it can filter down to my specific region.

Like
0
3 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
Every 3rd party tool local keyword search volume is wrong
There isn't a single lesson in the training about keyword search volume because no 3rd party tool gives data accurate enough to do anything with
The only way to get local search volume is to run Google Ads and back calculate it based on impression share and impressions. Even that's not accurate becuase it only gives a snapshot - unless you run a lot of Google ads
0

Ron Kwok
• Sep '25
oh ... can I trust the data that I am seeing on Trends.Google.com?

Elliot Berard
• Sep '25
@Ron Kwok You can, but trends measures the popularity of a query, not search volume.

It's very difficult to rank a GBP in a very large area because proximity is a factor

Core 30 is designed to establish primarily topical relevance

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/multi-location-question

Frank Apuzzo
Sep '25 • General discussion
Multi Location Question
@Caleb Ulku we have a client site that used its homepage as the gbp landing they also have several service pages optimized for the location. But, they’ve now added two more locations. Do we change from the home page as landing page even thou we have catergory and service pages in place ?

Jim and Frank
Like
0
3 comments
5
Elliot Berard
• Sep '25
@Frank Apuzzo Yes, create separate landing pages for each new location now that you have multiple GBPs.
Homepage stays as primary location landing page. Build dedicated landing pages for locations 2 and 3 with their own Core 30 structure.
If I'm understanding correctly, each GBP points to its specific landing page. Multi-service just means more service pages per location.
Location 1 = homepage
Location 2 = /locations/2-landing
Location 3 = /locations/3-landing

internal_all.csv
CSV • 99kb
links_all.csv
CSV • 12kb
Like
0
8 comments
5
Elliot Berard
• Sep '25
@Ron Kwok 106 pages without external links is like posting without anyone knowing. As Caleb and I have joked with each other: "If someone posts, but doesn't point a link, did they even post at all?"
Yes, you need one PBN link per page minimum. Without external links, Google sees your content as AI slop.
The red area at bottom looks like geographic boundary issue. Cosmetology schools have different ranking patterns than massage schools.
Get your links pointed and you should see the map improve significantly!
0
Reply
4
Stefan Girndt
• Sep '25
I think the main problem is that your GMB is located in Winter Park and you're targeting Orlando. For that, you're actually ranking pretty well, 4-6 in most places, so it should be possible even with the "wrong" city.
You are also looking at a huge area. Usually, I start with the default settings, which is a 133-pin circle, 1 mi apart. Your map seems to go out much further...
When you look at your competitors, find out how the ones with an Orlando address are doing compared to the ones outside Orlando.

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/homegrown-pbn
2
Ron Kwok
Sep '25 • SEO
Homegrown PBN.
I own several sites. Can I post content on these sites that will meet Google's requirements for PBN? I have 100 pages that I need to generate content for and only four sites to spread out the 100 articles. Do you think it is realistic to put 25 pages in each of the four sites? It does look weird to have 25 pages of posts all pointing to various pages on the same site.
TARGET SITE with 100 pages: hbsorlando.com
Sites that I own / can post articles to: betterskinmagazine.com, clearskinjournal.com, clearlybasics.com and tryclearly.com
Like
0
4 comments
4
Stefan Girndt
• Sep '25
Well, Google's main requirement for PBNs is that they shouldn't exist ;-)
So if Google can make a connection between all your sites (same GSC, logged into WP from the same IP, etc.) it won't help but might harm you.
0
Reply
5
Elliot Berard
• Sep '25
@Ron Kwok Do NOT use your own sites to link to your main business. That creates an obvious footprint and violates Google's guidelines.
Your existing sites should stay focused on their current purpose. Using them as a linking network to your cosmetology school is risky and could get everything penalized.
Remember that PBN stands for Private Blog Network. It takes many sites to create one valuable PBN without a footprint.
0
Reply
2
Ron Kwok
• Sep '25 (edited)
ok. So I need to buy a lot of ice cream!
1
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
Hah yeah what Stefan and Elliot said. PBNs are pretty grey hat. Quite a few people hate them and don't use them, which is fine - no shade thrown
If there's a footprint so that Google suspects the same person owns the PBN as owns the sites their linking to, they're likely to de-index the entire network.
Footprints: Hosting ip addresses, domain registrars, sites they're linking to, etc etc
Basically anything Google can see needs to be different across a PBN
0
Reply
1




2
Homegrown PBN.


===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/getting-links-right
3
Lalita Sundari
Sep '25 • SEO
Getting links right
Please may I have some help? I'm understanding there is three types of links:
Outbound Links: authority sites like wikipedia, forbes, etc
PBN links: Links that you buy to link to your website
Local Links: Chamber of commers, local charity, festivals, etc.
Is this correct?
If it is. For my services page, category pages, and content pages I understand I need:
Two oubound links, and one PBN link per page?
Is this correct?
Like
1
5 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
I mean if you include outbound authority links, then you might need to add internal links also
Generally, when we speak about links we're talking about external links pointing back to your site. That is the PBN link and trust links
One outbound link is often enough
But don't forget to place internal links to other pages you create
0
Reply
3
Lalita Sundari
• Sep '25
@Caleb Ulku I think im missing something.😭 im not sure what is trust Link? Is that local link? Or the Forbes Wikipedia Links?
Right now I have the internal
Linking. From home page ->category -> service -> category ->home page
I also have outbound authority links from the 100 links list: Forbes, Wikipedia, etc
I'm confused about the blogs links and the PBN Link. I buy this but not out them on the website right?
I'm about to buy from the ice cream shop, I hear you mention it doesn't matter it I get th... See more
0
Reply
5
Elliot Berard
• Sep '25
@Lalita Sundari Trust links = local authority links (Chamber, nonprofits, youth sports).
You buy PBN links from Ice Cream Shop, but they point FROM the PBN TO your website pages.
Each Core 30 page needs one external link pointing to it. Start with one PBN link per page, then add trust links as budget allows!

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/hvac-in-cold-weather-area
1
Jared Twining
Sep '25 • SEO
HVAC in cold weather area
I have an HVAC client who does air conditioning repair in the summers, and then transitions completely to furnace repair in the winter. Both are an equal part of the business. What should I put in the title tag?
Like
0
2 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
The primary GBP category + city name
Your real question then is what should the primary category be
What are the competitors doing in your area?
Which accounts for most annual profits?
HVAC repair can help you rank for both, but then you won't rank as well for either if you'd put it as the primary
This is a common question and it's really answered by each business. I've seen around 50 / 50 going for air conditioning repair service and hvac contractor in cold weather areas
Clearly in the south, all the busiensses with hvac contractor are doing it wrong
3
Reply
1
Robert Walker
• Sep '25
I just got a plumber in Maine who also does HVAC. There is a "Heating Contractor" category and a "boiler supplier". He is obviously going plumber for the main category but added the others as secondaries.

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/gbp-question-for-real-estate-firm
1
Travis Matthews
Sep '25 • General discussion
GBP Question for Real Estate Firm
I have started working with a Real Estate Broker who opened her own brick-and-mortar office. She has one other agent in the office currently, who is also a partner in the firm but not a broker.
Her current GBP is (Broker Name) - (Business Name). She wants to focus more on her firm and wants to update it to either remove her name or change it to (Business Name) by (Broker Name). I hear conflicting information on this topic, so I thought I would post in the group for advice. Any guidance is greatly appreciated.
Like
1
3 comments
1
William Morgan
• Sep '25
I would build a new profile with a different address and phone. It is better to have two locations then take the chance of getting the one suspended. this is just my opinion I am not and expert, I am here to learn.
0
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
I'd suggest naming the office’s GBP after the firm only (exactly as it appears on signage). Create separate practitioner GBPs for the broker and the partner under their personal names. Don’t use “Business Name by Broker Name” unless that’s the real-world brand on the door and everywhere else.
Google says the GBP name must match the real-world name used on your signage/website—no added descriptors
2
Reply
5
Elliot Berard
• Sep '25
@Travis Matthews GBP name changes risk re-verification or suspension, but we also want the business name to exactly match what is on the website.
If you update it in one place, you need to update it everywhere. Otherwise, keep current verified name.
Businesses change their names or rebrand often. I can say whether this would trigger a recertification, but sometimes it does not. I wouldn’t be surprised with either outcome.
If it were me, I would focus on the system here until the client decides to pull the trigger. Communicate the timeframe to them, and the potential outcomes. :)
0
Reply
1




1
GBP Question for Real Estate Firm

Mike Stevens
• Sep '25
Me too @Caleb Ulku
0
Reply
4
Doug Page
• Sep '25 (edited)
In my opinion, Caleb has it right. It's imperative to focus directly on your home town as the primary as that's where the address is that you give to Google. So your home page needs to concentrate on your primary category+home city. Then your location pages become the home pages for the other cities. You need to build your strength from the center outwards. With your location solidly built, as you dominate your home area it will start to spill into the areas close by as you continue to build out the topical relevance and geo relevance phases.
1
Reply
3

PBN Links
Question: Do I have this concept correct? We use PBN's to help Google index a page. Is this correct? So if a page is already indexed, then it doesn't need a PBN link? Or do we do PBN links to all Core 30 pages? Asked differently, if I already have some of the Core 30 pages indexed, do I still need to get PBN links for them? Thanks so much....
Like
1
5 comments
4
Scott DeSalvo
• Sep '25
I think the idea is that if you create a new page and or update content, you get a new PBN link so the page gets crawled/re-crawled.
1
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
I've been asked that question quite a bit and honestly can't say for sure either way. We budget a PBN link for every page we create for our clients. It's been a long time since I"ve just published content with no link. Some on here have had success with it, but back in the day we always saw much more movement with a PBN link to new content =)

@Lalita Sundari all content is every new page you publish or existing page you make significant changes to :)

Website URL in GBP Dashboard
@Caleb Ulku When listing the website's url inside of the GBP dashboard, do you recommend us to use the website's regular url or the utm_source tracking url?
Like
1
2 comments
3
Kent MacWilliam
• Sep '25
I'm curious what Caleb will reply, but my take is that it doesn't affect SEO. The deciding factor is whether you are actually using this tracking in Analytics. If you're mostly tracking map rank and not using analytics much like me, then this won't matter. If you've got multiple marketing campaigns running then you should track it.
0
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
I don't like the tracking code unless you're actually using it. I prefer the clean URL. I don't have a good reason for this because as Kent said, I haven't seen a significant SEO difference
We mostly don't use that tracking in analytics so usually leave it off

• Sep '25

Following the wizard of Ai SEO [Caleb] :)) to a T the ZERO GPT scores seem to be getting lower for me using a few extra instructions in the files area of the project.

GBP CAT and SERVICES

Customer AVATAR
USP's (so you don't have to edit the prompt each time, you can just type there "provided in the instructions)

Great! We don't stress over getting the scores super low. 60% is the absolute limit, but under 40% we stop trying to lower it

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/internal-link-silo
2
Heinrich Taitl
Aug '25 • SEO
internal link silo
we try to maintain the link silo within a category, no matter whether primary or secondary categories. do footer links, for example, have a negative influence on this silo, because they are working on every page? if so, would it be better to leave them out?
Like
0
2 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
@Heinrich Taitl Footer links pass minimal authority anyway - they do not hurt your silo structure significantly.
Editorial body content links are what really matter for passing authority between pages. Footer links are just navigation convenience.
Do not overthink this. Focus on proper editorial links between category pages and service pages. That is what builds your internal linking hierarchy. =)
4
Reply
3

• Sep '25

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/repurposed-use-of-site-content
4
Scott DeSalvo
Sep '25 (edited) • SEO
Repurposed Use of Site Content
I am updating my site structure to follow Caleb's brilliant suggestions.
I am abandoning a lot of Blog posts, as they have no keywords, traffic or backlinks.
I DO, however, have a lot of Blog Posts and some Pages which have some combination of backlinks, traffic and/or keywords. But they do not neatly fit into the new structure.
Should I make them Blog posts? This would seem to lose me quite a bit of link juice these pages may have.
Most are pages which DO track Services in the new site structure. S... See more
Like
0
1 comment
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
Unless the site has way too many blog posts, I generaly wouldn't go through and delete pages
Instead, expand on them, make them helpful. With AI generated content, this is usually nearly as fast as just marking the page for deletion
As for the content that doesn't quite fit that has links / getting traffic, you can do one of two things
1) Leave them alone =) Having extra content like this isn't going to hurt you especially if it's getting traffic
2) Edit titles / H1s / content to match the structure
Most of my clients have existing content and most of the time we leave the existing stuff alone
0
Reply
1

Caleb Ulku

• Sep '25
@Jorge Poveda You are correct, do not manually clean up individual citations. That takes forever and has minimal impact.
LeadSnap super citations strategy handles this automatically. API pushes consistent NAP data to 60+ platforms simultaneously.

Scott DeSalvo

Core 30 Question
Right now I have one location. In the mid term, that will have to change.
Is it ok to build my main GMB page separate from my homepage? Will that hurt anything?
I am doing it to future proof the future building and so I do not have to change homepage structure/
Thoughts?
Like
1
3 comments
2
Billy Dunley
• Sep '25
great question. I have a similar client ... he has a location and is getting ready to add a second location. I was thinking about having the location page be his GBP landing page and then again for new site coming online as soon as he's verified. What do you think Caleb? thanks
0
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
@Scott DeSalvo Do not separate your main GBP from homepage for single location business.
Homepage is most powerful URL on your domain. Use it for your primary location.
When you add locations later, create separate landing pages for those. Keep main location on homepage.
Future-proofing is good thinking but homepage power is too valuable to waste. =)

Gilberto Laparra

Do Blogs Help Our SEO Strategy?
@Caleb Ulku I just wanted to ask how important it is to publish blogs for the website? Do they play a significant role in improving SEO?
Like
0
3 comments
5
Elliot Berard
• Sep '25
@Gilberto Laparra Blogs=Pages=URLs. When we talk about any of the three, we are talking about content. The only difference is the formatting, and even that is variable.
With that in mind we can reframe your question:
"How important is it to publish content for a website?"
Very important, but I also know you are speaking about doing it on an ongoing basis. Once ranked, most local SEO sites can be left alone. Unless another competitor hires an SEO company there probably won't be a lot of volatility.*
*Does not count for cities.
2
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
@Elliot Berard taking 73 words to say "blogs are just pages" when he could have said it in 5 =)
Elliot's right though. "Blogs" versus "pages" is just formatting.
What matters is whether the content builds topical or geographic relevance for your GBP.
Most local businesses do not need ongoing blog publishing. Core 30 gets you ranked, then you are done unless competitors step up their game.

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/do-i-need-hundreds-of-external-links
1
Michelle Adamo
Sep '25 • SEO
Do I need hundreds of external links?!
@Caleb Ulku Excellent training! I've learned some gems from you, some missing pieces, that have made a difference in results pretty quickly! I have a question about external links via Ice Cream Shop. I have hundreds of pages to get an external link for, if I'm following your recommendations correctly.
For instance, we just created a set of 15 exterior cleaning pages for one of my multi-location clients (15 different locations) x 6 different cleaning services = 90 pages. Are you saying each one of... See more
Like
0
3 comments
5
Elliot Berard
• Sep '25
@Michelle Adamo The number of external links is not as overwhelming as it seems.
Yes, we want every URL to have one external link pointed to it. This will mean 90 external links if that is the number of URLs you end up making.
Keep in mind that you won't be pointing them all at once (right?), and that the content won't be completed all at once.
The way @Caleb Ulku and I would do it is point the links as the content was complete. Since we worked on a monthly contract, that meant there were three or ... See more
1
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
@Michelle Adamo
@Elliot Berard covered it perfectly. You want to drip the links as content gets completed.
90 external links sound overwhelming but you are not doing them all at once. 15-20 links per month as pages go live.
One PBN link per URL prevents AI slop classification. Ice Cream Shop links work fine for this purpose. =)
2
Reply
1
Michelle Adamo
• Sep '25
@Elliot Berard @Caleb Ulku Thanks so much to both of you! 😀
