===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/second-gbp-location
2
Daniel Namie
Sep '25 • SEO
Second GBP location
I have a client that ranks well in Elkhorn but wants to rank in neighboring Omaha (currently no visibility there).
They own a building complex in an annexation zone where addresses can legitimately use either "Elkhorn" or "Omaha" as the city.
Thinking of setting up a second GBP using an unused suite that is right next to their current address with "Omaha" instead of "Elkhorn."
Will the city name in the address actually help with Omaha rankings, or will proximity signals override it?
Like
0
2 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
@Daniel Namie Do not set up a second GBP for the same business at the same location. That violates Google guidelines and risks suspension.
City name in address helps but proximity is still primary factor. You're still in the same geographic location.
Build geographic relevance through content instead. Create "Service Name Omaha" pages and get Omaha-area links.
Over-reliance on tricks of convenience has always come to bite me in the past. =)
1
Reply
2
Daniel Namie
• Sep '25
@Caleb Ulku Glad I asked here first! Thanks!
0
Reply
1

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/what-impact-do-gbp-opening-hours-have
3
Justin Bryce
Sep '25 • General discussion
What impact do GBP opening hours have?
Just wondering what impact the GBP opening hours has on search results?
I have clients who want to be available from 8am - 4pm.
If someone is searching at 9pm for a Painting Company, what impact will this have?
Like
1
2 comments
3
Joe S
• Sep '25
Google does consider business hours in real time. If someone searches for a service at 9 PM and the GBP says it closed at 4 PM, the profile may still appear in the map pack, but with a “Closed” label.
In competitive markets, Google may prioritize showing currently open businesses higher, especially if it thinks searchers are looking for immediate service (e.g., restaurants, urgent services).
For service businesses like painting companies, where people aren’t expecting immediate availability, being... See more
0
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
@Justin Bryce Depends on the service. I don't know many paint companies working at 9pm without a special request.
Google's only goal is to satisfy its users as well and smoothly as possible.
1
Reply
1

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/gbp-webpage-question
2
Iman Diaz
Sep '25 • SEO
GBP Webpage Question
Hello everyone finally got 82% of the SEO Course done!
I want to confirm I understood something correctly.
I am registering a new GBP and the website url I provide for said GBP would link to a location page on my site that has local business schema correct but that page doesn't have to be my home page or should it ?
I am confident that page has to have local business schema - think the part I wasn't 100% sure of was what url should that page be or can it be?
thanks for your help
Like
1
2 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
GBP should point to your homepage for single location businesses.
Homepage is most powerful URL on your domain. Homepage gets local business schema.
Only use separate landing pages for multi-location businesses where each GBP needs its own dedicated page.
Single location = GBP points to homepage with local business schema =)
3
Reply
2
Iman Diaz
• Sep '25
thank you so much - clears that up perfectly
1
Reply
1

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/link-juice-question-navigation-menus

Sharon Martin
Sep '25 • SEO
Link Juice Question (Navigation Menus)
In the lesson on How SEO Authority is Passed, Caleb mentions that links in the navigation menu pass no authority, and since the nav menu is the first link crawled, pages linked in the nav menu won't get any authority at all for internal linking.
I'm trying to verify:
Let's say you have a page about "Chicago SEO", if it's linked in the menu and you link it later on in the actual content, that content link would be invalidated for internal link juice?
There's nothing that can be done to offset the ze... See more

0
2 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
@Sharon Martin We aren’t ranking the website, we are ranking the GBP. Most users won’t ever read a word of your content.
This is a major rabbit hole.
1)- When was the last time you read all the content on a plumbers site? If you try calling a painter, how often do you read the words on the “house painting” page of a painters website?
2)- Footers can be affected, but they aren’t the first links on that pages, so we are less worried about them.
3)- These are a great question: what are users looking fo... See more

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/zero-gpt-pass-rate
4
Karan Holland
Sep '25 • General discussion
ZERO GPT Pass rate %?
What % do you let ZERO GPT go to when your posting content? under 60? or is it lower like 15-20% as in the Skool Video. Any help much appreciated
Like
1
1 comment
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25 (edited)
Target 60-70% human-written score on ZeroGPT. We often get it lower, but if it's higher than that we definitely spend time editing / reprompting to get it lower
It's more difficult in professional / medical fields as professional language typically triggers a higher score by default.

Kimberly Gile
Aug '25 • SEO
Catagory Landing Page Question/Clarification
When creating the landing pages for each of the 30 GBP categories/services - Are you all building these catagory/services pages as lead gen landing pages or information landing pages for optimizing your local search? I'm thinking the latter makes more sense (Informational) It seems the intent of a google will be the authority/relevance of the information on the page. The buyer intent would be captured from clicking on the GDP link which would route to the "home" landing page specific to that GBP making the "Home" landing page the lead gen page. Am I thinking about this correctly?
Like
1
2 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
@Kimberly Gile Correct. Service pages = informational for topical relevance. GBP clicks = homepage for conversions.
Service pages rank the GBP. Homepage converts the traffic. Two different jobs.
Focus on Core 30 first like you said. =)

Hi Vab, I'm assuming you have already done your Core 30, have green in the middle of your Rank Map, and are ready to move to the geographical relevance phase. Great job!
If not, you need to continue with PAA questions to get topical relevance. I'll assume you are moving on to the geographical relevance phase.

@Vab Rising geographic boundaries are important.
Google will set a hard limit on the distance past city limits where it will allow a GBP to rank.
@Matthew Furman Has the prioritization correct. If you haven’t dominated your local area entirely, then that is where I would focus.

Follow the rank map.

Chamber of Commerce Scam
I believe I was scammed. Coincidentally, right around the time I was learning to join a Chamber of Commerce, I received an email from https://www.essexchamber.org/ to join. $220 seemed about right from what I learned. So impulsively, while driving, I paid it :)
I setup a listing, there is a website, yet the same emails have not stopped, continuously. I emailed telling them I am already a member, please stop emailing. No response!
They did a lot of things right to seem legit, even fooled ChatGPT.

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/ai-image-generation
2
Michael Shavers
Aug '25 • SEO
AI Image generation
Anyone in here have any experience using AI generated images for use with core 30 content?
Does Google frown on these images?
In my niche, we work extensively with people for which we would need to obtain written permission to photograph them in the services we provide. Needless to say, this not logistically feasible.
From what I've read, stock photos should be avoided.
This leaves us with using AI generated images.
Like
0
6 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
@Michael Shavers Google doesn’t know they are AI, and I use AI photos.
You are correct that you don’t want to use stock photos if you can help it.
1
Reply
2
Michael Shavers
• Sep '25
@Caleb Ulku Have you ever taken a stock photo and had AI regenerate it? Does Google still consider that to be "Stock?"
0
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
@Michael Shavers That isn't how AI generation works. =)
AI regeneration uses tools such as inpainting or text-to-image to create new pixel data entirely, so Google would likely see it as unique content rather than stock.
Both AI tools generate fresh pixels instead of copying original data. Google's reverse image search looks for exact or near-exact matches, not conceptual similarity.
That said, use real photos when possible. AI images still look artificial and may hurt user trust, especially for local businesses where authenticity matters. =)
0
Reply
2
Victor Sammarco
• Sep '25
Lately we’ve been using Google lab Whisk. Whisk is pretty powerful. You can upload different context reference photos, type in your ai prompt and select the size. Then boom, in less than 5 seconds it’ll generate a pretty realistic image. I do recommend using chatGPT to describe a very detailed image of what you want and then take that prompt and put it into whisk to produce the image.
1
Reply
2
Michael Shavers
• Sep '25
@Victor Sammarco Thanks for the tip. I'll check it out.
0
Reply
2
Victor Sammarco
• Sep '25
Oh, and it’s completely free.
0
Reply
1




Jump to latest comment
2
AI Image generation

Axel Hilland

New GBP or keep the old
I have a client who is going to start a bar. There is already a GBP from a previous bar at the location. Is it better to create a new GBP, or to change the name of the one that’s already there?

8 comments
1
Sharon Martin
• Sep '25
Are the reviews on the bar positive? Also Google may choose to suspend it if you try the entire name change. Typically a brand new business would get a new business profile. Now if the new bar owners bought the rights to the old bar, maybe you could make a case for continuing the profile.

2
Victor Sammarco
Sep '25 • SEO
To migrate or not to migrate?! Stuck.
I own an established Wix website that generates significant monthly revenue. For years, I've wanted to migrate it to WordPress to unlock better functionality and performance. However, I'm deeply concerned about one critical risk: will the migration destroy my top 1-2 Google search rankings?
This website means a lot to me, so I'm extremely cautious about making any move that could jeopardize its search visibility. I'm looking for real-world experiences from others who've made similar migrations. D... See more
Like
0
9 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
@Victor Sammarco So long and you remap the URLs correctly and 301 redirect everything correct, you will be able to save ~80-90% of your authority.
My old business partner, Elliot, migrated Gold’s Gym’s website last year and surmised that SEO is about growth, but migrations are about retention of what you have.
If it were me, so long as the site is ranking, I wouldn’t migrate it without an extremely good reason for doing so.
What is the ROI of moving it to Wordpress?
2
Reply
2
Victor Sammarco
• Sep '25

Well, the return would be much greater because we would be able to do much more in terms of scaling. Adding backend CRM, plugins, update the website, etc.
I like that you had mentioned so long that the remap of the URLs are done correctly and 301s, I can still get this done.
If it's ~80-90%, that's still good to me! @Caleb Ulku

@Victor Sammarco I've rebuilt sites when I knew it would make or save money.
Now, I do want to point out that I said 'authority', not rank position or traffic. I wouldn't be able to tell what effect the loss of the authority would have until I saw data afterwards.
Migrations are exercises of diligence. =)

Will Schatz
• Sep '25 (edited)
I'd add that ideally initially you'd keep most things similar to how they are on the wix site - title tags, h1 and subheads, paragraph text, general page structures, alt text on images, etc. Then make sure that internal links are all updated to reflect new URLs (if any). Keep URLs the same if you can, and if you can't, then 301s are a must. Try to update links from other sites as well (links from other websites or social accounts you have, links from other websites if you have connections there), if the URL being linked to on your site changes.

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/image-optimization
1
Gilberto Laparra
Sep '25 • SEO
Image Optimization
@Caleb Ulku I just wanted to know if it’s okay to use a JPEG photo on the pages if the size is smaller than a WebP file, or if it's still better to use WebP? Also, as a follow-up, is it fine not to use geo-tagging in photos?
Like
0
1 comment
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
@Gilberto Laparra Use the smaller file size format regardless of whether it is JPEG or WebP. Page speed matters more than specific image format.
For geo-tagging: yes, geotag images before uploading to GBP. Use GeoImgr or similar tool to add location metadata. Skip geo-tagging for regular website images - waste of time for minimal benefit.
Also, you are asking me if I think it is a good idea to not follow the methodology I teach. Geotags are stripped, but Google still reads it. =)
1
Reply
1




1
Image Optimization

$500-1000/month total sounds right for your budget.
PBN links for all 10 pages: ~$350 ($35/each)

Real authority links (Chamber, youth sports) move rankings. PBNs just prevent AI slop classification.

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/gbp-categories
4
Karan Holland
Sep '25 • SEO
GBP Categories
This client i have it wont let me add more categories, Any help be much appreciated
Like
2
4 comments
1
Juan Velasco
• Sep '25
Google won’t let you add categories that aren’t already predefined in their GBP system. That’s why you’re seeing those red boxes, the categories you typed in don’t exist in their database.
You’ll need to select categories from the drop-down suggestions that appear when you start typing. Unfortunately, you can’t create your own custom categories. The best approach is to choose the closest match from Google’s available list (e.g., “Electrician,” “Electrical installation service,” “Contractor”) to cover the services your client offers.
3
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
@Karan Holland It like exactly like what @Juan Velasco said: you can’t add categories that don’t exist.
The example I use is Mold Remediation.
Mold remediation company uses “Water Damage Restoration Service” as GBP category because “Mold Remediation” does not exist.
Create webpage titled “Mold Remediation [City]” but use H1 “Water Damage Restoration Service [City]” to match GBP.
The H1/title tag approach maintains GBP consistency while targeting what people actually search for. Standard re-theming practice. =)
3
Reply
4
Karan Holland
• Sep '25
@Caleb Ulku Really helpful thank you
0
Reply
2
Abe K
• Sep '25 (edited)
Work with Google with what they give you as noted above and related categories that matter which Google provides. Don't fight and attempt to swim upstream on this key thing as it will be very tough battle that you are guaranteed to loose 100% of the time.
1

@Michelle Adamo If you don’t want to sell those as stand alone services, then I suggest sticking with Painter and Painting categories. Do not add pressure washing, carpenter, drywall, etc.

Kimberly Gile
Aug '25 • SEO
Local City name different from city target
Hello! I'm a newbee and also not an agency. We own veterinary hosptials and I've joined the group to fix issues that we couldn't figure out even after using multiple agencies for both our ads and SEO. I am STOKED to be here! Learning a ton. I have a basic question for the SEO groups here. Our address (phoenix) and our target city (Paradise Valley) are different. What is the best way to handle that? I am getting advice from the LLM's to keep Phoenix as the address in our GBP and use Paradise Valley in all our content to help google show in the map pack in paradise valley.
Like
1
5 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Sep '25
@Kimberly Gile I'm glad you are here. That LLM advice is absolutely terrible and could destroy your GBP rankings entirely!
You are suggesting location deception: keeping Phoenix address but targeting Paradise Valley content. At best, it won't help you rank in your target city due to geographic boundaries. At worst, Google will see this mismatch and could suspend your business profiles for false location information.
With three vet hospitals, we don't want any suspensions. =)

As Caleb teaches, all we are doing is match the GBP structure. Within the GBP, services under primary category are listed and described directly under the primary category. 👑
• Aug '25
@Chukuneye Neye-Akogo Very well put. The main landing page is the most important category, and the services within it are placed on the page optimized for that service. In this example, it would be the landing page.

2
3 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
@Joshua MacFall Use "Dallas" for homepage. Most people search "junk removal Dallas" not "junk removal DFW" or the full metroplex name.
Pick your primary city and stick with it. Do not try to target three counties from one homepage - that dilutes your focus.
All that said, SAB without visible address is going to be tough regardless. 30-50% more effort than businesses with physical addresses. =)

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/multi-location-business-closed-the-main-location
4
Brian McDaniel
Aug '25 • SEO
Multi location business closed the main location
I have this client for a few years in Wellington, FL. No problem, GBP Category Wellington FL. Then he opened another location Miami. No problem, location page GBP cat Miami.
Then he moves Wellington to West Palm Beach. OK, change Wellington to WPB, no problem.
Now he is dumping WPB and keeping Miami. Miami has been a battle, but we are seeing results at last. Miami GBP has always pointed to the Miami page.
What to do with the WPB home page? Just call it Florida? Leave it alone? Just build all pages in the future to link to the Miami page and use Miami for all of them?
Like
0
4 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25 (edited)
@Brian McDaniel If it were me, I would point homepage to Miami since that is your only location now.
Do not make it generic "Florida" - that does not match any actual GBP. If there is value then 301 redirect the pages, otherwise I would let them 404.
Build all future Core 30 content around Miami location. If the Miami page is ranking, keep using it as your landing page.
Simple rule: Landing Page should match your primary GBP location and category. One location = one homepage focus.
That said, what a frustrating client. I'm glad he is understanding of what you have been doing. =)
0
Reply
4
Brian McDaniel
• Aug '25
@Caleb Ulku ok, that makes sense. GBP landing page remains the same (website .com/miami) optimized for gbp primary cat + Miami. Home now switches from WPB to Miami and all supporting pages switch to Miami. Should I update internal linking to the home page that used to use Wellington or WPB in the anchor?
Yeah, frustrating client, but also one of those clients I never hear from, he just pays the bill. So not too frustrating. Lol
0
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
@Brian McDaniel At this point I would ask myself if I wanted to just treat the client's website as a multi-location site.
Personally, I don't like the idea of touching things that are working, so siloing each location's Core-30 would allow you to keep the Homepage content from changing again in the future.

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/primary-category-supporting-services
5
Mark Farrow
Aug '25 • General discussion
Primary Category - Supporting Services
For the Main Page - the Primary GBP Cat, where do we put the services pages for that main Cat? Are those the critical 1-3 that link direct? There are 40 services for my Primary Cat.
Like
0
1 comment
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
@Mark Farrow 40 services under primary category is too many. Group related services together.
Primary category services get listed on your Services hub page in navigation, along with secondary categories. Services page links to your most important/profitable service pages directly.
Do not create 40 separate pages. Group similar services: "Emergency plumbing," "Water heater services," "Drain cleaning," etc.
It can be easy to feel the need to rank for each variation of a service, especially when they... See more

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/gbp-account-opening
2
Andri Werren
Aug '25 • General discussion
GBP Account opening
Just to be super clear: When registering a new GBP, what type should we now use to avoid service area business?
It is for a cleaning company which has an actual office address. Therefore, should I use local store instead of the Service Business to avoid the hard time to get ranked as a service area type.
Like
0
1 comment
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
@Andri Werren Use "Service business" for cleaning company - that is what you actually are.
The key is showing your physical address, not hiding it. Service Area Business (SAB) problems come from HIDING the address, not from being a service business.
Local store is for retail businesses customers visit, like restaurants or shops. Cleaning companies go to customers, so you are a service business.
Keep your office address visible and you will be fine. =)
1
Reply
1




2

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/master-schema-list
3
Alec Neumeyer
Jul '25 • SEO
Master schema list
Has anyone made a master schema list that shows what schema should go on what page for a local service busienss?
Like
2
12 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
LocalBusiness schema and service schema on the GBP landing page
Organization and website schema everywhere
FAQ schema where applicable
That's basically a minimum that we implement.

Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
@Becky DeGrossa No, I would use Service schema on service pages. Schema allows me to convey information to Google as fast as possible. =)

Reply
2
Heinrich Taitl
• Aug '25
@Caleb Ulku in line with the topic, here's another question: on a 2nd category page with n-services (briefly described and linked to the service page). which schemas belong on this category page? also the service schema or not?
0
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
Organization schema on every URL minimum. That is the baseline.
For category pages listing multiple services, you can add service schema for the main category service, but do not overcomplicate it.
LocalBusiness schema specifically only goes on the URL your GBP points to. Keep it simple - organization schema plus maybe service schema if it makes sense.
Do not spend hours perfecting schema when you could be building more Core 30 pages. =)

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/link-prioritization
2
Daniel Namie
Aug '25 (edited) • SEO
Link prioritization?
For clients whose budget doesn't allow for purchasing local links and backlinks at the same time in the first few months, which should I prioritize first while building their Core 30 pages: Securing chamber of commerce links, or Building the Core 30 backlinks?
Also, is it okay to publish all Core 30 pages upfront and then purchase the back links spread over 3-4 months, or should I avoid publishing any page without an accompanying backlink?
Like
1
8 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
@Daniel Namie Publish Core 30 pages first, then add links over 3-4 months. Do not wait to publish.
Prioritize basic PBN links for all Core 30 pages before Chamber links. Need to prevent AI slop classification on every page.
Chamber links are authority builders but basic indexing comes first. Authority links work better once foundation is solid. =)

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/fb-ad-when-to-decide-its-not-working
2
Joshua MacFall
Aug '25 • SEO
FB Ad - when to decide it's not working?
Hello! I'm new here and glad to meet you all. I created this ad based on the YT video Caleb made and did my best to set it up for HVAC using all of the recommended settings. Now that I'm here in the Pro subscription, I see in the training video that Caleb's team has spent over $7.5k on the ad.
I'm curious, has anyone else run thee ads, and when did you start seeing real results? The one lead I got was spam. :(
Like
0
6 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
Generally if you've spent more than 3x of the expected result, the ad isn't working
So if you want $30 per booked call -- once you've spent $90 with no booked called, it's a sign to change the ad.
Usually if it "doesn't work" the problem is with the offer.
$88 with one lead is definitely in the range of "something is wrong"
Check out the FB ads course in the classroom here - a lot more details in the setup versus what was in the YT video
Make sure the links all work and the form fills all work properly. Make sure the pixel is set up correctly
1
Reply

Switching to Ad Manager will give you more control over these ads. I'd recommend you add an offer and focus on a certain location. For example, "I'll get you 10 new AC Service calls in the next 30 days or I work for free if I don't" or something along those lines that gets people excited.

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/sab
3
Lalita Sundari
Aug '25 • SEO
SAB
Hello,
I addes a service area to my business google profile unknowling of the downsides, but it the profile also does have a physical address. I just deteled the services area but I was wondering if it less of a problem to rank if you have both, an addres and a service area?
Like
1
4 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
Service areas in the GBP have almost nothing to do with ranking.
A service area busienss or "SAB" means one that doesn't have a visible address. Those are very hard to rank.
I would strongly suggest adding a visible address to your GBP if SEO is a goal
2
Reply
2
Rob Hosking
• Aug '25
@Caleb Ulku What is a business called that is not an SAB, and is it next to impossible to rank one of those in the map pack without a physical address?
Thanks.
0
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25 (edited)
@Rob Hosking Businesses with visible addresses are just called "local businesses" or "storefront businesses."
If you have NO physical address visible, that IS a Service Area Business (SAB).
Takes 30-50% more effort to rank than visible address businesses.
Always show physical address if you have one. Much easier to rank than SABs. =)
Nothing is impossible. Hole in ones occur, lotteries are won, and it isn't impossible for you to do a slam dunk with a basketball without warm up.
It's just very hard.
1
Reply
View 1 more reply
1




Jump to latest comment
3

@Lalita Sundari Welcome! Do not split services by time duration. That creates artificial pages that nobody searches for.

The Hierarchical Structure
Your site structure follows a three-tier hierarchy:
1. Topic Level (Tier 1)
Start with 30 main topics based on your target keyword
Each topic represents a broad subject area within your niche
These serve as your pillar content pages
2. Variation Level (Tier 2)
Create 15 variations for each topic
These are more specific angles or subtopics
Total: 450 pages (30 topics × 15 variations)....

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/strategy-to-drive-more-calls
3
Alec Neumeyer
Aug '25 • SEO
Strategy to drive more calls
Hi there, I've been following the course for awhile now and implementing as best as I can and am seeing heatmaps change but in certain instances I'm not seeing the number of calls increase. I'm not sure what could be off on our attempt here but I'd of course like to drive calls a bit more aggressively instead of heatmap reporting as the way to show value. Any advice?
Like
0
2 comments
3
Kent MacWilliam
• Aug '25
Once the heat map goes green you’re ranking. The next decision the buyer makes when they see you in the map pack is which of the 3 to call. The best way to beat the other two is # of reviews. How do your review numbers compare?
1
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
@Alec Neumeyer @Kent MacWilliam is correct, reviews solve this issue almost all the time.
Beyond that, could be search volume issue. Not enough people searching for the service in that location. Obvious example: ranking well for "snow removal Miami."
Check actual search volume for your keywords in that area. Green heatmap does not help if nobody searches those terms. =)

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/slug-paths
3
Christian Wert
Aug '25 (edited) • SEO
Slug Paths
@Caleb Ulku
I am working on a General Contractor website.
Here are the categories:
General Contractor (Primary) (Homepage)
Remodeler (Secondary)
Home Builder (Secondary)
I will have about 6-7 service pages under each category.
I am wondering about the correct way to do the slug paths here. the secondary categories are easy because they come with an interior slug path.
Example secondary category paths:
domain.com/remodeler/home-remodeling/
domain.com/home-builder/custom-home-construction/
Since the primary ... See more
Like
1
1 comment
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
If there is a page already indexed and ranking, do not change the slug paths. As I often say, don't touch it if it's ranking.
URL structure does not matter. Site structure is built through internal linking, not URLs.
Every GBP service gets exact matching webpage. Focus on proper internal links between category and service pages, not perfect URL path structure.
As long as every service has a page and links properly, you are following Core 30 methodology. =)
1
Reply
1




3
Slug Paths


===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/add-services-under-each-category
3
Christian Wert
Jul '25 • SEO
Add Services Under Each Category?
Here are my categories:
Primary: Real estate agent
Secondary: Real estate consultant, Real estate agency, Property consultant, Commercial real estate agency, Industrial real estate agency
Should I use the services prompt to add services under the secondary categories? Should services be repeated across categories, or should each service appear only once? I'm also noticing that some secondary categories are being suggested as services for others. For example, "Real estate agency" appears as a sugges... See more
Like
2
5 comments
2
Elizabeth Nguyen
• Jul '25
Services should not be repeated across categories, each service should only appear once.
For example, since every category or service gets its own page, you will have one URL for /real-estate-agency and /real-estate-consultant. That means you wouldn't have a category page for real-estate-agency and service page for real-estate-agency as that means there would be a double up.
2
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
@Christian Wert @Elizabeth Nguyen is correct. Each service gets one page only. No duplicates.
Don't overthink the GBP organization. Core 30 = one page per service, regardless of which category it's under. =)
0
Reply

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/gbp-help
1
Bill Van Valer
Aug '25 • General discussion
GBP Help
Good morning all. We have been trying to get our new office location verified through google for the last couple of weeks. We have submitted copies of the lease, done a video verification showing the sign on the building, opening the door with the key and walking through the office, but it still says "in review". Now this morning I woke up to another location (we have 3 currently) getting suspended. Not sure where to go from here. From past experience it seems like if we appeal the suspension an... See more
Like
0
1 comment
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
@Bill Van Valer Good news - you do NOT get only one shot at appeals. That's wrong information.
You get initial appeal, then if denied you can request "additional review" with more evidence. Two separate chances.
Current reality: Appeals taking 4-6 weeks instead of 5 days due to suspension surge since January. Google is catching up but still slow.
Do NOT touch your working third location while sorting this out. Leave it completely alone.
Moving suspended GBP to new location is possible but risky - transfers often fail and you lose everything. =)
2
Reply
1




1
GBP Help

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/performance-based-contract-for-client-with-existing-gbp-and-website-generating-leads
2
Lukas Halim
Aug '25 • General discussion
Performance Based Contract for client with existing GBP and website generating leads
How would you approach a performance-based contract for a client with an existing GBP and website that was already generating leads? Prospect this morning has two GBP profiles for his business in different geographic areas, and they are generating some calls but they haven't been optimized recently. Options I can think of are:
1) Pivot to a flat fee pricing model
2) Do a baseline period before making changes to observe the number of calls he's currently getting
Like
0
1 comment
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
Baseline period first. Track calls for 30 days before touching anything.
"Don't touch it if it's ranking" - need to know exactly what's working before you optimize.
After baseline, flat fee is much easier than performance contracts. Too many variables you cannot control with existing businesses. =)
1
Reply
1




2

2
Axel Hilland
Aug '25 • SEO
Leadsnap keywords searchgrid
When tracking keywords in leadsnap, is it main catagory+city or do you only track main catagory?
psychologist oslo, or only psychologist
the results in in the rank map shows a huge difference between the two serch terms
Like
0
5 comments
4
Stefan Girndt
• Aug '25
It's easiest to rank for "category city" than "category near me" than "category".
I would start with "category city" until you rank well and then add "category near me".
I would ignore "category" because that is not specific enough for local searches.
1
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
We track 'category city.' Just 'category' doesn't always trigger a map result
The 'near me" as Stefan said increases the importance of proximity in the search so it's harder to rank farther from the GBP. Typically we only target 'near me' after 'category city' is ranking very well, then we'll build additoinal trust and geo for the near me dots
0
Reply
2
Axel Hilland
• Aug '25
@Caleb Ulku I’ve noticed that searching by category alone always seems to trigger a map result, but with more difficult rating. If I live in Houston (your favorite city) and just type in ‘electrician,’ shouldn’t that give me pretty much the same results as if I typed ‘electrician Houston’?
0
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
@Axel Hilland nope. Google is Google and has data showing people searching without the city are looking for something different than with the city.

Caleb Ulku

@Muhammad Abdullah Without topical relevance, you get geographic visibility without service clarity.
Geographic pages first means you rank in new areas, but Google does not know what services to show you for.
You might rank well for "River Oaks" searches, but Google needs the Core 30 service pages to understand you are a plumber.
To answer your other question:
Your client is overthinking this. Show them the rank map improvements from the geographic pages you already built. Results speak louder than ... See more

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/pages-not-appearing-as-indexed-in-google-search-console
3
Justin Bryce
Aug '25 (edited) • General discussion
Pages not appearing as Indexed in Google Search Console
I have a client with 37 pages in the sitemap.xml
When I look at Google Search Console (GSC) there are only 26 pages indexed, and I don't see the 11 pages missing from the Sitemap in the 'Not Indexed' section of GSC.
Is there a way to tell which pages are not indexed?
I've reviewed the Not indexed section and check the Why pages aren't indexed.
This is not showing the missing pages.
Like
1
3 comments
3
Justin Bryce
• Aug '25
update - the Sitemap was last reviewed 26th April so I submitted it again.
However still keen to see if we can tell which pages are not indexed from sitemap.
0
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
Not from sitemap, but coverage. A sitemap is just a signal to google about what pages are important. It doesn't strictly follow it.
2
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
The index coverage inside the search console will list all the URLs Google has found, whether they're indexed, and if they aren't indexed why not. If they aren't appearing in the index coverage, Google hasn't found them yet. Do they have internal links? Do they have external links? If yes to both, then consider using a service like indexmenow.com
2
Reply
1




3

Question About Deliverables


===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/gbp-category-vs-higher-volume-keyword-which-should-i-target
2
Andri Werren
Aug '25 • SEO
GBP Category vs. Higher-Volume Keyword: Which Should I Target?
@Caleb Ulku Quick question about keyword tracking and Google Business Profile (GBP) categories:
If my client’s primary GBP category is “Cleaning service”, but they want to target “Cleaning company” (which has higher search volume but there's no it's own GBP category), should I track both keywords? Does Google treat these two terms as semantically the same? Also, what’s the best practice here — should I focus on the GBP category keyword or the higher-volume keyword?
Lastly, should I create separate... See more
Like
1
1 comment
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25 (edited)
@Andri Werren The goal of the methodology is to achieve parity between the GBP and the website.
Keyword search volume doesn't matter as much with local. SEO tools are more accurate the more data they have. Most towns and cities have little hyperlocal data so keyword tracking tools are notoriously incorrect.
Don't force a category because of search volume. Focus on what would give the fastest ROI right now. You will rank for both terms.
2
Reply
1




2
GBP Category vs. Higher-Volume Keyword: Which Should I Target?


===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/dedicated-homepage
2
Victor Sammarco
Aug '25 (edited) • SEO
Dedicated homepage?
For local businesses operating in multiple locations, what would be the homepage?
Some businesses I've come across have already established their homepage as a generic page with no location associated with it in the content, while having location pages as inner pages. The location pages are the GBP landing pages.
I believe this was built out purposely because the client wants to expand and not have their homepage strictly focused on a single location with location-targeted keywords.
Based on our training, what do we do as SEO's in this situation? @Caleb Ulku
Like
1
2 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
@Victor Sammarco Homepage can stay neutral for multi-location businesses. Each GBP gets its own dedicated landing page as inner pages.
Do not change what is already working. If the current website is ranking well with current structure, leave it alone.
"Do not touch it if it is ranking" applies here. Focus on optimizing what needs improvement instead. Add, don't subtract. =)

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/gbp-location-not-showing-in-branded-search
1
Saif Saab
Aug '25 • SEO
GBP location not showing in branded search
Hi again – following up now that I'm on AI SEO Mastery Pro, I'm hoping to get more in-depth help.
We had asked about a case where a newly launched branch of a restaurant (~3 months old, 470+ reviews, 4.8 stars) is completely overshadowed in branded searches by the original location (4 years old, 3,300+ reviews). The two branches are in two different cities (1,000 miles apart), and both Google Business Profiles are active and verified.
When users in the new city search for the brand name on Google ... See more
Like
2
2 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
@Saif Saab What is happening is something called 'filtering'. Google will actively filter things from Google Maps so the user isn't overwhelmed with dots.
Let me break it down for you: Google sees the two locations, but the old location is the one it is sending users to because it knows its users like it, so it is safe.
Right now Google does not know as much about your current location. They may both be the same franchise, but in this case your client is competing with themselves.
Since that is the... See more
2
Reply
1
Saif Saab
• Aug '25
@Caleb Ulku Thank you for the detailed explanation; it clarified a lot.

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/client-offer-in-light-of-website-ranking-time
3
Peter Ball
Jul '25 • SEO
Client offer in light of website ranking time
@Caleb Ulku On the call yesterday (July 1) you mentioned that it could take six to 9 months to rank a new website.
First, by “website“ do you mean the actual website or are you referring to the GBP or both?
Second, if it takes that long, what kind of an offer am I making a client that needs a website given that many clients may well be reluctant to keep paying without any results for that long?
Like
3
9 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
New domain or new GBP will usually take 6-9 months unless there's almost no competition. We had a plumber in eclectic, AL rank the day after their GBP was created, but that isn't typical. Google hates new things.
Usually we offer a "geographic reservation fee" that includes the website build and assitance with the GBP. Then they don't start paying again until they actually start receiving real leads as calls.

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/gbps-for-different-cities
2
Rob Hosking
Jul '25 • SEO
GBP's for different cities
Hey @Caleb Ulku, one of my clients has a home clinic in one city and another pop up one in another city and there is one GBP for each. Should I append the city name to the end (or change them in another way) so people know which one to go to for reviews etc., or will Google automatically direct people to the closest one to them?
One has Renee in the name and the other has Renée, to distinguish them apart (and Renée is the correct spelling), but when I type in "... Renee" into Google, it goes to the closest one to me, which is the Renée one, which is what I want.
Like
0
12 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
@Rob Hosking Google automatically shows the closest location to the searcher. That is working correctly.
Do not add city names to business names - Google does not like keyword stuffing. Keep the real business names.
Different spellings might cause confusion. Pick one consistent spelling for both locations. =)
1
Reply
2
Rob Hosking
• Jul '25
Ok thanks! That clears it all up...

Moving a website away from GHL
When you built a website on GHL for a client and he later wants to leave you and your services but wants to keep the website (because he paid for it).
How would you get it off of GHL?
Like
1
6 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
@Stefan Girndt For that question I would contact GHL support. Website migrations are commonplace.
If it were me, I would send over handover instructions/links to the client and unlock the domain to be transferred. It is their website and they paid for it. This is part of winding down the engagement.
2
Reply
4
Stefan Girndt
• Aug '25
@Caleb Ulku the problem is that there is no default technical option to migrate a GHL website to another platform.
I was wondering if someone has a certain solution for this.
Letting the client get their own GHL account and transfer the subaccount over would be an easy way but also quite expensive and technicologically challenging for them.
0
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
@Stefan Girndt How difficult it is for them is not your issue.
As you said, having them create an account is the easiest way for you to transfer everything to them.
Do that for the domain name as well and you are done. Their new marketing agency would be responsible for the migration of the files. Your job is just to tell them how to get their website.

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/external-linking-to-site-urls
2
Benjamin Shannon
Aug '25 • General discussion
External Linking to site URLs
If I’m sourcing chamber links, youth sponsorships or directory links should they point to the corresponding city/location page or the homepage? In the past I’ve always thought to point to the homepage to build max authority but after re-watching some training videos it got me thinking they should be linked to the relevant location pages or geo locations if they have been created.
Any guidance or wisdom is appreciated 🙏🏻
Like
1
2 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
@Benjamin Shannon Link to the specific location page that matches the geographic area of the link source.
Chamber of Commerce in Phoenix should link to your Phoenix location page, not homepage. More relevance equals better authority boost.
Homepage gets authority through internal linking from all location pages. Geographic matching is more powerful. =)
0
Reply
2
Benjamin Shannon
• Aug '25
@Caleb Ulku makes sense, thanks for clarifying.
0
Reply

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/local-seo-setup-for-multidisciplinary-clinic-gbp-landing-page-structure
2
Axel Hilland
Aug '25 • SEO
⚙️ Local SEO setup for multidisciplinary clinic – GBP & landing page structure?
I’m working on local SEO for a multidisciplinary clinic with:
Doctor
Chiropractor
Physiotherapist
Osteopath
Naprapath
Personal trainer
On-site gym
All work at the same location under the same brand.
Main questions:
Google Business Profile:– Only one physical location– How would you choose the primary category?– How to make sure no profession feels overlooked, while still optimizing visibility?
Landing page structure: What is best practice in this scenario?
Like
2
1 comment
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
@Axel Hilland Choose the most searched profession as primary category. Usually "Medical clinic" or "Physical therapy clinic" depending on your area.
Add other professions as secondary categories. Use services section for all treatments offered by each practitioner.
Single GBP pointing to homepage optimized for primary category. Create individual practitioner pages for each specialty.
Simple structure, do not overthink it. =)
2
Reply
1




Jump to latest comment

Destynnie Berard

@Becky DeGrossa Usually this is the services overview page. This should be on the website; you're essentially saying "Hey, we suggest you upload this link to your website to this section of the gbp". Similar to suggesting they add their booking link.

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/menus-how-they-effect-interlinking-architecture
2
Christian Gladwell
Aug '25 (edited) • SEO
Menus - How they effect interlinking architecture
When running screaming frog, I noticed that it detects the menu as well
Example:
-A Homepage has a menu, so the homepage links to various other pages listed in the menu
Does this mean that I should keep the menu very small?
Or maybe a mega menu would be better to literally interlink everything with every page?
Does this also mean that the menu should only have no-follow tags?
Navigation links don't pass authority, as the course explains, so maybe we don't have to worry about nofollow tags in menus
Also... See more
Like
0
2 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
@Christian Gladwell This is all answered in the training. We keep the navigation nice and organic, only showing the pages that we need to.
Also, not all businesses are created equal, so maintain flexibility. Your measure of success is an ROI for the businesses you are working with. Don't overthink this.
1
Reply
3
Destynnie Berard
• Aug '25 (edited)
Screaming Frog crawls all navigational links, same as Google, but Google doesn't really place a ton of weight on nav menu or footer links. But this is how it navigates your site once the crawler is on there.
I'm not sure if you can ask SF to specifically not crawl these links, but you can dive in deep to what it does/doesnt crawl in the Configuration settings.
1
Reply
1

Christian Gladwell

Should I worry about crawl depth?
I have a client that has his location pages 6 folders deep
website.com/data/medical/dropdownmenu/locations/location1
Should I restructure the site in order to reduce crawl depth? Or just ignore it?
Like
0
5 comments
3
Jeremy Bengtson
• Jul '25
I’d say it’s a good idea to rethink that site structure with the crawl depth issue. Having six folders deep, like website.com/data/medical/dropdownmenu/locations/location1, can make it tough for search engines to crawl it properly, kind of like a customer trying to find a dentist in Austin at dentistinaustin.com/locations/austin-downtown buried under a bunch of extra steps. John Mueller from Google once mentioned that a flatter site structure helps search engines find and index content better, a... See more
1
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
@Christian Gladwell Google does like wide and shallow websites.
Are each of the /*/ pages indexed or have an index tag?

I used screamingfrog for this URL string, and it was not indexable, also didn't find it on google search index, and also screaminfrog says there's a 301 redirect(yet the page doesn't redirect anywhere different, weird).

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/technical-audits-not-necessary-for-websites-with-under-500-urls
2
Rob Hosking
Jul '25 (edited) • SEO
Technical Audits not necessary for websites with under 500 URLs?
Hi @Caleb Ulku, you said in your Technical Audit video that you only do a technical audit for a client's website if it's larger (for local SEO/map pack ranking). Is it only worthwhile for a smaller website when it's in the building authority stages of the website, if it's relatively new, or is it ever not worthwhile for smaller websites? Thanks!
Like
1
2 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
@Rob Hosking For local SEO, technical audits rarely matter on small sites. Focus budget on Core 30 content and authority links instead.
Only worth it if site has obvious technical problems preventing indexing. Most small local sites do not need technical audits.
You will have better ROI building topical and geographic relevance instead. =)

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/client-impersonation-website-need-advice
2
Daniel Namie
Jul '25 • General discussion
Client Impersonation Website - Need Advice
Just discovered someone created a fake website impersonating one of my clients.
Phone number on the site goes to an H&R Block message (clearly bogus)
Contact form is questionable
Using my client's branding/identity
Has anyone dealt with client impersonation sites before? What worked best for takedowns?
Any SEO-specific concerns I should address?
Like
2
4 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
@Daniel Namie
Contact the hosting provider with DMCA takedown request and report to Google for impersonation. Document everything with screenshots
Check ICANN database for registrar details. Most respond quickly to impersonation reports. =)
2
Reply
2
Daniel Namie
• Jul '25
@Caleb Ulku Thanks Caleb! I submitted a DMCA takedown to webflow (site is built on a webflow subdomain). I also submitted a the report to Google.
After a bit of digging I found out the site is owned by my client's old marketing company. I am not sure if they are being malicious or they are trying to win him back with a new site and accidentally submitted it to be indexed.
2
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
@Daniel Namie Google likes it if you make an effort to contact the site owner. Have your client reach out to their old marketing company and request their GBP to be transferred. The owner can always provide evidence that they are the business owners to Google.

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/menus-how-they-effect-interlinking-architecture
2
Christian Gladwell
Aug '25 (edited) • SEO
Menus - How they effect interlinking architecture
When running screaming frog, I noticed that it detects the menu as well
Example:
-A Homepage has a menu, so the homepage links to various other pages listed in the menu
Does this mean that I should keep the menu very small?
Or maybe a mega menu would be better to literally interlink everything with every page?
Does this also mean that the menu should only have no-follow tags?
Navigation links don't pass authority, as the course explains, so maybe we don't have to worry about nofollow tags in menus
Also... See more
Like
0
2 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
@Christian Gladwell This is all answered in the training. We keep the navigation nice and organic, only showing the pages that we need to.
Also, not all businesses are created equal, so maintain flexibility. Your measure of success is an ROI for the businesses you are working with. Don't overthink this.
1
Reply
3
Destynnie Berard
• Aug '25 (edited)
Screaming Frog crawls all navigational links, same as Google, but Google doesn't really place a ton of weight on nav menu or footer links. But this is how it navigates your site once the crawler is on there.
I'm not sure if you can ask SF to specifically not crawl these links, but you can dive in deep to what it does/doesnt crawl in the Configuration settings.
1
Reply
1




2
Menus - How they effect interlinking architecture

Menus - How they effect interlinking architecture
When running screaming frog, I noticed that it detects the menu as well
Example:
-A Homepage has a menu, so the homepage links to various other pages listed in the menu
Does this mean that I should keep the menu very small?
Or maybe a mega menu would be better to literally interlink everything with every page?

Navigation links don't pass authority, as the course explains, so maybe we don't have to worry about nofollow tags in menus
Also... See more
Like
0
2 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
@Christian Gladwell This is all answered in the training. We keep the navigation nice and organic, only showing the pages that we need to.
Also, not all businesses are created equal, so maintain flexibility. Your measure of success is an ROI for the businesses you are working with. Don't overthink this.
1
Reply
3
Destynnie Berard
• Aug '25 (edited)
Screaming Frog crawls all navigational links, same as Google, but Google doesn't really place a ton of weight on nav menu or footer links. But this is how it navigates your site once the crawler is on there.
I'm not sure if you can ask SF to specifically not crawl these links, but you can dive in deep to what it does/doesnt crawl in the Configuration settings.
1
Reply

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/should-i-worry-about-crawl-depth
2
Christian Gladwell
Jul '25 • SEO
Should I worry about crawl depth?
I have a client that has his location pages 6 folders deep
website.com/data/medical/dropdownmenu/locations/location1
Should I restructure the site in order to reduce crawl depth? Or just ignore it?
Like
0
5 comments
3
Jeremy Bengtson
• Jul '25
I’d say it’s a good idea to rethink that site structure with the crawl depth issue. Having six folders deep, like website.com/data/medical/dropdownmenu/locations/location1, can make it tough for search engines to crawl it properly, kind of like a customer trying to find a dentist in Austin at dentistinaustin.com/locations/austin-downtown buried under a bunch of extra steps. John Mueller from Google once mentioned that a flatter site structure helps search engines find and index content better, a... See more
1
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
@Christian Gladwell Google does like wide and shallow websites.
Are each of the /*/ pages indexed or have an index tag?

So I moved down the subfolders and checked all of them
I used screamingfrog for this URL string, and it was not indexable, also didn't find it on google search index, and also screaminfrog says there's a 301 redirect(yet the page doesn't redirect anywhere different, weird).
https://www.gastromaryland.com/contents/our-locations/locations/... See more

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/technical-audits-not-necessary-for-websites-with-under-500-urls
2
Rob Hosking
Jul '25 (edited) • SEO
Technical Audits not necessary for websites with under 500 URLs?
Hi @Caleb Ulku, you said in your Technical Audit video that you only do a technical audit for a client's website if it's larger (for local SEO/map pack ranking). Is it only worthwhile for a smaller website when it's in the building authority stages of the website, if it's relatively new, or is it ever not worthwhile for smaller websites? Thanks!
Like
1
2 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Aug '25
@Rob Hosking For local SEO, technical audits rarely matter on small sites. Focus budget on Core 30 content and authority links instead.
Only worth it if site has obvious technical problems preventing indexing. Most small local sites do not need technical audits.
You will have better ROI building topical and geographic relevance instead. =)

Like
0
2 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
@Rob Hosking Do not let competitor page counts intimidate you. Most of those pages are not optimized for local rankings.
Build your Core 30 structure first. Quality topical relevance beats quantity every time.
"Skin care clinic" vs "skin cancer clinic" are different searches entirely. Stick with your actual category and build proper geographic relevance around it.
Google uses the entire website for authority signals, but local rankings depend more on GBP connection and local content quality. =)

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/client-impersonation-website-need-advice

Daniel Namie
Jul '25 • General discussion

Phone number on the site goes to an H&R Block message (clearly bogus)
Contact form is questionable
Using my client's branding/identity
Has anyone dealt with client impersonation sites before? What worked best for takedowns?
Any SEO-specific concerns I should address?
Like
2
4 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
@Daniel Namie
Contact the hosting provider with DMCA takedown request and report to Google for impersonation. Document everything with screenshots
Check ICANN database for registrar details. Most respond quickly to impersonation reports. =)
2
Reply
2
Daniel Namie
• Jul '25
@Caleb Ulku Thanks Caleb! I submitted a DMCA takedown to webflow (site is built on a webflow subdomain). I also submitted a the report to Google.
After a bit of digging I found out the site is owned by my client's old marketing company. I am not sure if they are being malicious or they are trying to win him back with a new site and accidentally submitted it to be indexed.
2
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
@Daniel Namie Google likes it if you make an effort to contact the site owner. Have your client reach out to their old marketing company and request their GBP to be transferred. The owner can always provide evidence that they are the business owners to Google.

Steve Mizer
• Jul '25
Hi @Jeff Garner! Yeah Google can be a pain when it comes to GBP delisting. I find another thing that helps is to update the content on your site before updating the listing.
If it's a relatively new listing (such as a new location), try not to make too many changes for a while.
But if absolutely necessary, try and create a good amount of off-site documentation about the changes before you edit your GBP.
Examples:
Update all your citations first.
Get a press release published about the new location, ... See more

services underneath primary category
Hi Caleb,
In your course ‘How to Structure the Core 30’, you show in the graphic that several service pages should be created under each category. Like
0
5 comments

Caleb Ulku

• Jul '25
@Heinrich Taitl Homepage IS the GBP landing page for single location businesses. They are the same page.
Multi-location businesses get separate GBP landing pages. Primary category services link from the GBP landing page, not necessarily homepage.
Internal linking creates hierarchy, not URL structure. =)

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/do-you-guys-track-your-clients-new-backlinks
3
Jason Leibler
Jul '25 • SEO
Do You Guys Track Your Client's New Backlinks?
Like when you have them sign up for their chamber of commerce, sponsor events, and then all the links you purchase for them. Do you track whether all those actually get indexed and they get "credit" for them?
If so, what program do you use to track?
I know there are plenty of programs that can do it (SEM Rush, Ahrefs, Moz, etc). But is there a way to track without having to pay for one of those? I know you can see some link info in Google Search Console.
Or is that a total waste of time, and you just order the links and that's it?
Like
0
6 comments
3
Alec Neumeyer
• Jul '25
Yes. I was saving the backlinks in a google spreadsheet but we moved to Clickup so instead I made a custom field for "backlink url" and we add it directly to the task to create the page.
Then I can pull a dashboard pretty easy of the custom field and see what links I got for each customer.
1
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
@Jason Leibler Ahrefs for quick checks, but do not overthink it.
Order the links, they will help. The PBNs in the ICT shop don't give you a domain link at all. Focus on getting more authority links instead of tracking every detail.
My old partner, Elliot, would say: "The proper way to use a tools like Ahrefs is to look at the data, then let your eyes fuzz out. What trends do you see?"
Analysis paralysis territory. =)
3
Reply
3
Jason Leibler
• Jul '25
@Caleb Ulku sounds good.
Out of curiosity, why don’t the PBN’s give a domain link?
0
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
@Jason Leibler to protect the network.

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/should-homepage-be-location-based-or-category-based
2
Clovis Shropshire
Jul '25 • General discussion
Should homepage be location based or category based?
When decided the target keyword for a homepage, should it be based on the location or category, then have location based pages?
For example, "dumpster rental" or "dumpster rental orlando"?
Like
1
5 comments
1
Guy Skoy
• Jul '25
For Page Title (That shows up in Google Search) use something like this:
Dumpster Rental Orlando, FL | [Your clients business name here]
For H1 use "Dumpster Rental Orlando, FL" and then your client specific details.
1

2
Clovis Shropshire
• Jul '25
@Guy Skoy thanks a lot that's very helpful!

Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
@Clovis Shropshire @Guy Skoy's right. Homepage targets primary category + city.
For single location businesses, homepage = GBP landing page. "Dumpster Rental Orlando" format is correct. =)

@David Jullien
Word Count: Write for Google first. Check what's ranking for your keywords - if they're 1500+ words, match it. UX doesn't matter if you're not ranking.
Page Layout: Don't overthink design. Simple layout, focus on content that ranks. Perfect is the enemy of good.
Geographic Results: a) Position swaps are normal. If important areas improved, you're winning. b) Stubborn spots = strong local competition. Add more authority links to that area. c) Too granular can dilute. Stick to meaningf... See more

Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
First, what is success to the client and where does the majority of their revenue come from? That will let you know local vs, National.
If it is local, then the answer to your second question is: No, you aren’t making a Core 30 for all those locations. You will make a Core 30 for a single GBP until it dominates and then move to another.
Warehouse that they own count as physical locations. I would get the GBPs verified.

Question About GBP Management and Related Services
1) When you are doing local SEO for a client, are you also handling their GBP management -- like the weekly posts, adding pictures, and responding to reviews? Or do you expect them to do those things?
2) Does anybody sell standalone GBP management (like the things mentioned above) for a small monthly fee?
3) Does anybody sell "one time GBP optimization" where you set up everything perfectly for them in their GBP, but you aren't actually doing local SEO for them?
Like
0
1 comment
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
@Jason Leibler I handle GBP optimization as part of Core 30 setup, not ongoing management. If they need to upload the photos I would do it for them, just in case.
Posts/photos don't impact rankings much. Difficult to report on KPIs. Focus budget on content and authority links instead.
One-time GBP optimization makes sense. I had a 'Kick-Off Package' in the past which was basically a one time set up fee, audit, some keyword research and roadmap forward. Ongoing management is mostly busy work. =)

Alec Neumeyer

Yes. I was saving the backlinks in a google spreadsheet but we moved to Clickup so instead I made a custom field for "backlink url" and we add it directly to the task to create the page.

7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
@Jason Leibler Ahrefs for quick checks, but do not overthink it.
Order the links, they will help. The PBNs in the ICT shop don't give you a domain link at all. Focus on getting more authority links instead of tracking every detail.
My old partner, Elliot, would say: "The proper way to use a tools like Ahrefs is to look at the data, then let your eyes fuzz out. What trends do you see?"
Analysis paralysis territory. =)

Guy Skoy

For Page Title (That shows up in Google Search) use something like this:
Dumpster Rental Orlando, FL | [Your clients business name here]
For H1 use "Dumpster Rental Orlando, FL" and then your client specific details.

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/core-30-structure-for-business-that-doesnt-have-services-in-gbp
3
Becky DeGrossa
Jul '25 • SEO
Core 30 Structure for Business That Doesn't Have Services in GBP
Hey @Caleb Ulku - I'm drinking up your course to see what I can apply to my audience -- psychologists, psychotherapists, counselors -- and the occasional Psychiatrist or Coach.
I'd love to tell you what I'm seeing and then what I *think* I should test -- and get your feedback on it if you're game!
Our client's business don't have the option to list Services in GBPs. To get our client's "specialties" and "modalities" in the GBP, we've been using the Product feature in the GBP and then creating thes... See more
Like
0
3 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
@Becky DeGrossa, sounds like a perfect re-theming situation.
First, add "Mental Health Service" or "Psychotherapist" as secondary GBP category.
Then, create /mental-health-service-denver page.
Title tag: "Depression Therapy Denver" H1: "Mental Health Service Denver" Content focus: Depression therapy specifically
Same concept as car accident lawyer → civil law attorney. We teach Google what we mean by specific overlapping secondary categories. =)
1
Reply
3
Becky DeGrossa
• Jul '25 (edited)
@Caleb Ulku what do I do if I have more therapy specialties than available categories to re-theme? I'm assuming the re-theming is a 1 for 1 play?
0
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
@Becky DeGrossa If you have more specialties than categories, then I would suspect that several of those categories are services in disguise. In that case, it comes down to prioritization.
Google will understand what is going on as you continue to add service pages.

@David Jullien
Word Count: Write for Google first. Check what's ranking for your keywords - if they're 1500+ words, match it. UX doesn't matter if you're not ranking.
Page Layout: Don't overthink design. Simple layout, focus on content that ranks. Perfect is the enemy of good.
Geographic Results: a) Position swaps are normal. If important areas improved, you're winning. b) Stubborn spots = strong local competition. Add more authority links to that area. c) Too granular can dilute. Stick to meaningf... See more

7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
First, what is success to the client and where does the majority of their revenue come from? That will let you know local vs, National.
If it is local, then the answer to your second question is: No, you aren’t making a Core 30 for all those locations. You will make a Core 30 for a single GBP until it dominates and then move to another.
Warehouse that they own count as physical locations. I would get the GBPs verified.

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/question-about-gbp-management-and-related-services
3
Jason Leibler
Jul '25 • SEO
Question About GBP Management and Related Services
1) When you are doing local SEO for a client, are you also handling their GBP management -- like the weekly posts, adding pictures, and responding to reviews? Or do you expect them to do those things?
2) Does anybody sell standalone GBP management (like the things mentioned above) for a small monthly fee?
3) Does anybody sell "one time GBP optimization" where you set up everything perfectly for them in their GBP, but you aren't actually doing local SEO for them?
Like
0
1 comment
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
@Jason Leibler I handle GBP optimization as part of Core 30 setup, not ongoing management. If they need to upload the photos I would do it for them, just in case.
Posts/photos don't impact rankings much. Difficult to report on KPIs. Focus budget on content and authority links instead.
One-time GBP optimization makes sense. I had a 'Kick-Off Package' in the past which was basically a one time set up fee, audit, some keyword research and roadmap forward. Ongoing management is mostly busy work. =)

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/core-30-structure-for-business-that-doesnt-have-services-in-gbp
3
Becky DeGrossa
Jul '25 • SEO
Core 30 Structure for Business That Doesn't Have Services in GBP
Hey @Caleb Ulku - I'm drinking up your course to see what I can apply to my audience -- psychologists, psychotherapists, counselors -- and the occasional Psychiatrist or Coach.
I'd love to tell you what I'm seeing and then what I *think* I should test -- and get your feedback on it if you're game!
Our client's business don't have the option to list Services in GBPs. To get our client's "specialties" and "modalities" in the GBP, we've been using the Product feature in the GBP and then creating thes... See more
Like
0
3 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
@Becky DeGrossa, sounds like a perfect re-theming situation.
First, add "Mental Health Service" or "Psychotherapist" as secondary GBP category.
Then, create /mental-health-service-denver page.
Title tag: "Depression Therapy Denver" H1: "Mental Health Service Denver" Content focus: Depression therapy specifically
Same concept as car accident lawyer → civil law attorney. We teach Google what we mean by specific overlapping secondary categories. =)
1
Reply
3
Becky DeGrossa
• Jul '25 (edited)
@Caleb Ulku what do I do if I have more therapy specialties than available categories to re-theme? I'm assuming the re-theming is a 1 for 1 play?
0
Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
@Becky DeGrossa If you have more specialties than categories, then I would suspect that several of those categories are services in disguise. In that case, it comes down to prioritization.
Google will understand what is going on as you continue to add service pages.

Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
@Alec Neumeyer is right. "Near me" is the hardest milestone - requires more authority and time.
Small town still needs geographic pages. "Fence contractor Highway 395 Boardman" or major intersections/landmarks in your area.
"Near me" rankings come after you dominate keyword + location. Keep building authority links and geographic content. =)

@Kyle Huggins You are correct here. =)
I have experimented with the relevant content existing on a different domain that we were trying to rank at the time.

What did work was the creation of a brand new website about the services they provide, complete with a Core-30. We then:
Linked to the client's franchise page
Added schema to the homepage declaring their franchise profile link as part of the Structured Data
Added a link to their brand new website within the content of the Franchis... See more

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/google-business-verification
1
Dustin Keeslar
Jul '25 • General discussion
Google business verification
Can someone help me understand why there is such a complex process for GBP verification?
I was able to verify my clients business with their phone number. It says verified in the GBP screen where I see all my businesses.
Am I missing something? Is there a higher tier of verification you you get with the whole video process? Why go through that much work?
Like
0
5 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
@Dustin Keeslar Scammers creating multiple business listings to drown out the competition. This lowers trust in Google Search and Google Ads, costing Google money. Google is incentivized to bring the most accurate and helpful results it can.
1
Reply
1
Dustin Keeslar
• Jul '25

Reply
4
Stefan Girndt
• Jul '25
@Dustin Keeslar if your GBP already is verified, you don't need to do another (video) verification. In case your listing gets suspended, you might need to do the video verification but until then (hopefully never), you're good.

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/keyword-location-vs-keyword-near-me
3
Bob Foerster
Jul '25 • SEO
<keyword> <location> vs <keyword> near me
So, here's the conundrum.
Nobody searches for our keyword + location (shown by keyword planner and our search console data). It's always 'keyword near me'.
I had never run the keyword + location scan til today (and we did a ton of core30 work recently). This is actually pretty awesome, but it doesn't seem relevant since nobody searches this way.
How do we get the actual 'near me' searches to boost our relevance? Any ideas?
Do I need to put 'near me' on our pages? Bad one is 'near me', better one is 'location'
Like
0
2 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
@Bob Foerster Don't add "near me" to your pages. Google uses location data, not the text "near me."
If your Core 30 is solid, "near me" rankings should improve too. Google knows your location relevance from GBP connection and local content.
Focus on Core 30 fundamentals. Don't overthink the "near me" vs location difference. =)
2
Reply
3
Bob Foerster
• Jul '25
@Caleb Ulku Ok, thanks. Was shocked to see how well the location search turned out already.
Backlinks is the next push I need for local proof, working on it.

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/does-listing-prices-with-services-help-ranking
2
Bruce Fleck
Jul '25 • SEO
Does listing prices with services help ranking?
Does it help with ranking to list the prices with services? Or is it just a convenience for the customer?
Most of my clients are reluctant to list prices because they feel it locks them in. However, as a consumer, I like the price transparency.
I'm sure it varies also by industry. Listing prices for plumbing services is different than listing prices for cosmetic surgery.
Thoughts?
Like
0
1 comment
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
@Bruce Fleck Interesting question!
Pricing doesn't directly impact rankings. It's user experience and conversion optimization.
Adding services with descriptions helps relevance, but pricing is optional. Focus on completeness - categories, services, reviews matter more.
Your clients are right about pricing flexibility. Most successful local businesses don't list prices in GBP.
Better to focus on Core 30 and authority links. =)
0
Reply
1

@Christian Wert If you are asking about a modifier as in Schema, context, and H1/H2 tags, then yes.
If you are asking about the URL structure, it won't have an effect. Internal linking creates site structure, not Slugs.

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/clarification-on-gbp-categories-services
3
Chris White
Jun '25 • SEO
Clarification on GBP Categories + Services
@Caleb Ulku / anyone who can help...
I just got a roofing client. My first client. Woohoo!!
I'm using GMB Everywhere as a tool to help with Categories and Services.
My client focuses on (1) Residential Roofing, (2) Commercial Roofing, and (3) Solar Installation.
GMB Everywhere is suggesting the categories in the screenshot below. The only two I'm seeing in the screenshot below that seem feasible are: (1) Roofing Contractor and (2) Roofing Service.
The only category I see populating in the GBP is "Roo... See more
Like
1
7 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jun '25
@Chris White "Roofing Contractor" as primary is correct.
Add these secondaries that actually show up in GBP. Note that these are not available everywhere:
General Contractor
Siding Contractor
Construction Company
Solar Panel Installation Service
Ignore what tools suggest if the categories don't exist in GBP. Use what's actually available. For example, don't add "Gutter Cleaning Service" unless they actually clean gutters. Same with others.
What services does your client actually provide? Build your categories around that, not what tools think you should have.
3
Reply
3
Chris White
• Jun '25

@Chris White roofing contractor is/should be your primary. You are correct roofing services is not an option unless something has changed. 99% of roofers are using roofing contractor.
If gbp offers a solar category that should be your #2 based on what I’ve heard you say in calls.
Do they offer siding? My roofer does a lot of siding and has siding contractor as a secondary category.
Then gutters if applicable and windows. Obviously make sure your customer offers these services and know that based on... See more

Dru W

@Caleb Ulku I can dig it! Thank you. Ended up posting to /blog.
For these FAQ support articles, I have only linked back to the product page.

I am aware of the impact of interlinking on a site in general. And understood that building these topical funnels around the FAQ, were explicitly to be interlinked between each other, and not the rest of the sit... See more

Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
@Dru W Link the FAQ articles to each other in a circle too.
Article 1 → Article 2 → Article 3 → Article 1. Plus they all link up to the main page.
Main page links down to all three with short intro paragraphs.
That's the full structure. =)

Brian McDaniel

I like dentists because it is so straightforward. Gbp primary cat is….dentist! Teeth whitening service and a few others, depending on their services. Its just so simple. They have the money, especially if they are established.
Med spa is a bit different. Same simple easy category and some nice subcats, but the owners have been a bit flaky in my experience. Maybe I just got the bad ones. They are cheap and want to rank for everything else.

===POST=== /ai-seo-mastery-pro/core-thirty-service-page-structure
1
Shawn Jones
Jul '25 • SEO
Core thirty service page structure
Example: category page is water heat repair houston-sevice category for this is water heater replacement.
Question: target keyword water heater replacement Houston or water heater replacement in Houston. Does it matter if "in" is left out. Trying to avoid keyword stuffing.
Like
0
1 comment
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
Your target keyword is water heater replacement houston
Including the in is perfectly fine in the title tag and h1. You need to include the exact match target keyword - so you'll definitely want other words in there beyond the exact match keyword

Would you leave them be, remove them, or... See more
Like
0
4 comments
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
This is weird. I can't imagine it helping or hurting much. That many links with identical anchor text isn't awesome, but again it's not like you're trying to rank the contact us page.
Honestly, I'd probably just leave it alone... maybe let the client know they're wasting their
time? It definitely isn't helping. And if it's hurting, it's only hurting by a tiny amount and not worth your team's efforts to go through and fix it.

4
Brian McDaniel

@Caleb Ulku that was pretty much my thoughts on it too. If he was linking to the home page, I would certainly want to stop it, but its just the contact page. I am just going to mention it the next time I send him content.

Reply
7
Caleb Ulku
👑
• Jul '25
@Brian McDaniel I've always framed the conversation as, "I noticed that you often link to the Contact Us page. As your [SEO Title], I would advise against this."
Then I give a brief explanation on how large or small the issue is. If the issue is large, then prioritize it. If it is small, why did I bring it up in the first place?
Were you going to ask him to remove the links, leave them be, or ask him to not add them 'going forward'?
